v.t: Carolingian Renaissance reaches its peak
v.s: From the late 8th to early 9th century, the Frankish king Charlemagne vigorously promoted a cultural revival to consolidate his rule and train bureaucratic talents. He recruited scholars from across Europe, including Alcuin of York and Paul the Deacon of Italy. Charlemagne ordered the construction of churches, monasteries, and palace schools, promoted the clear and legible Carolingian minuscule script, and organized scholars to copy and preserve numerous classical Latin texts. This revival not only rejuvenated classical scholarship but also facilitated the unification and spread of Christian doctrine, laying the foundation for later medieval European civilization.

v.t: Du You composes the 'Tongdian' (Comprehensive Institutions)
v.s: Tang chancellor Du You spent over thirty years and completed the 'Tongdian' in 200 volumes in 801 AD. It is the first comprehensive institutional history of China, covering from the mythical Yellow Emperor to the Tianbao era of Tang, divided into nine sections: food and goods, elections, official posts, rites, music, military, penal law, prefectures, and border defense. Du You had served as military governor, and his work became a model for later institutional histories.

v.t: Byzantium promulgates the 'Legal Handbook'
v.s: To address local judicial chaos, Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I issued the 'Legal Handbook' in 801, simplifying clauses from the Justinian Code to make them more applicable to rural and provincial courts. The handbook integrated Christian ethics, emphasized protection of the weak, and regulated the selection of judges. Nikephoros' reforms temporarily alleviated judicial injustice within the empire but faced resistance from large landowners.

v.t: Charlemagne sends envoys to Byzantium
v.s: In 801, Charlemagne dispatched a mission to Constantinople to discuss marriage with Byzantine Empress Irene, seeking recognition of his imperial title through the union. The envoys brought gifts of jewelry, weapons, and ivory artifacts. However, Irene was deposed in 802, and the marriage plan failed, though trade and diplomatic contacts continued.

v.t: Tang frontier military governors (jiedushi) continue expanding power
v.s: In his later years, Emperor Dezong adopted a policy of appeasement toward the jiedushi, who not only controlled military power but also appointed their own staff and collected taxes. By 801, the three northern circuits – Chengde, Weibo, and Lulong – all had hereditary rule, with the court unable to intervene. The jiedushi imposed heavy taxes and raised private armies, forming de facto independent kingdoms. This fragmentation severely weakened central authority and set the stage for future rebellions.

v.t: Vikings establish a trade network from Northern Europe to the Caspian Sea
v.s: In the 8th and 9th centuries, Viking merchants traveled widely from Norway to the Caspian Sea, trading amber, furs, and slaves for Arab silver, silk, and spices. Around 801, Viking trading centers like Birka and Hedeby became important hubs. Using flat-bottomed ships for river navigation, they facilitated the commercialization of the East European plain and indirectly contributed to the formation of the Rus' states.

v.t: Jayavarman II establishes the Angkor dynasty
v.s: In 802, Jayavarman II held a sacred ceremony on Mount Kulen, proclaiming himself 'Universal Monarch' and declaring Cambodia's independence from Java. He founded the Angkor dynasty, establishing his capital at Hariharalaya (today's Roluos temple complex). Jayavarman II introduced the 'Devaraja' (god-king) cult, merging Hindu worship of Shiva with kingship, laying the foundation for the six-century rule of the Khmer Empire.

v.t: Byzantine Empress Irene is deposed
v.s: In 802, Byzantine finance minister Nikephoros launched a palace coup, deposing Empress Irene and proclaiming himself emperor as Nikephoros I. Irene had alienated the aristocracy due to her marriage plans with Charlemagne and iconophile policies. Nikephoros immediately consolidated finances, repealed Irene's tax cuts, and strengthened control over the church, bringing temporary stability to Byzantium.

v.t: Pyus musical troupe arrives in Chang'an
v.s: In 802, King Yongqian of Pyu (present-day Myanmar) sent his son Shu Nantuo leading an ensemble of over thirty to Chang'an, performing twelve pieces of Pyu music. They used unique instruments such as the phoenix-headed harp and snail-bucket mouth organ, performing Buddhist-themed dances. Bai Juyi later wrote a poem titled 'Pyu Music'. This cultural exchange enhanced Tang understanding of Southeast Asia, and Pyu music became one of the ten court ensembles of the Tang.

v.t: Battle of Hadisa in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 802, the Abbasid army under Caliph Harun al-Rashid defeated Byzantine reinforcements near Hadisa in Syria, consolidating Arab control over the northern frontier. The battle was triggered by a border chieftain rebellion supported by Byzantium. Afterward, Harun forced thousands of Greek residents to relocate to Baghdad and strengthened fortifications in Antioch and other military towns.

v.t: Charlemagne issues the 'Imperial Decree'
v.s: In 803, Charlemagne issued an imperial decree in Aachen requiring all free men in the empire to swear loyalty to the emperor, and ordering counts to regularly inspect local areas and report judicial cases. The decree also standardized weights, measures, and currency, with silver coins bearing Charlemagne's portrait. This strengthened centralization, though local nobles implemented it poorly, limiting its effectiveness.

v.t: Tang frontier governors intensify private tax collection
v.s: In 803, the jiedushi of the three northern circuits arbitrarily added new taxes, such as 'roof tax' and 'land fee', using the revenue to support private armies. Envoys sent by the court to offer reconciliation were denied entry. Emperor Dezong tolerated this, only issuing mild admonitions. This fiscal independence strengthened the centrifugal tendencies of the frontier governors, and the court's actual revenue from prefectures under its control sharply declined.

v.t: First official paper mill established in Baghdad
v.s: In 803, under the patronage of Caliph Harun al-Rashid, the first official paper mill was established in Baghdad, using craftsmen captured from Samarkand. The Arabs improved Chinese papermaking techniques, using flax and rags instead of tree bark to produce cheaper, smoother paper. The spread of papermaking reduced the cost of books, promoting the Translation Movement and Islamic scholarly flourishing.

v.t: Byzantium and the Franks negotiate over the status of Venice
v.s: In 803, the Byzantine Empire and Charlemagne held diplomatic talks in Aachen over the ownership of Venice and the Istrian peninsula. Charlemagne demanded Byzantine recognition of his imperial title in exchange for Frankish withdrawal from disputed territories. The negotiations ended inconclusively, but Venice remained nominally under Byzantine suzerainty while being effectively independent.

v.t: Saichō and Kūkai travel to Tang to study Buddhism
v.s: In 804, the Japanese monks Saichō and Kūkai crossed the sea with the Japanese envoy mission. Saichō studied Tiantai Buddhism from Daosui on Mount Tiantai, later founding the Japanese Tendai sect; Kūkai received esoteric transmission from Huiguo at Qinglong Temple in Chang'an, mastering both the Womb and Diamond realms, and upon return founded the Shingon sect. They brought back numerous sutras and ritual objects, contributing to the independent development of Japanese Buddhism.

v.t: The Arab Translation Movement flourishes
v.s: In 804, under Caliph Harun al-Rashid and his son al-Ma'mun, the House of Wisdom in Baghdad undertook large-scale translations of Greek, Persian, and Indian classics. The translator Hunayn ibn Ishaq spent ten years rendering Ptolemy's 'Almagest' into Arabic. The Translation Movement introduced Greek rationalism to the Islamic world, providing intellectual sparks for the European Renaissance.

v.t: Charlemagne's empire reaches its greatest extent
v.s: In 804, Charlemagne completed the conquest of Saxony, executing the rebel leader Widukind, and pushed the eastern border of the empire to the Elbe River. The same year, he forced the Duke of Bavaria to surrender and annexed his territory as a royal province. Charlemagne's empire stretched from the Atlantic in the west to the Danube in the east, south to central Italy, and north to the North Sea, becoming the most powerful political entity in Western Europe.

v.t: Rise of the Kilwa Sultanate on the East African coast
v.s: Around 804, Persian immigrants from Shiraz established settlements on Kilwa Island, which later developed into the Kilwa Sultanate. They used monsoon winds to trade gold, ivory, and slaves, competing with Indian and Arab merchants. This city-state became an important node on the East African coast, marking the beginning of Swahili culture.

v.t: The Two Wangs and Eight Sima incident
v.s: In 805, after Emperor Dezong's death, Emperor Shunzong ascended the throne. He supported the 'Yongzhen Reforms' led by Wang Shuwen, Wang Pi, and eight others including Liu Yuxi and Liu Zongyuan. They abolished corrupt palace markets and dog catchers, reduced eunuch power, and dismissed redundant officials. However, the reforms angered eunuchs and provincial governors. In the eighth month, eunuch Ju Wenzhen staged a coup, forcing Shunzong to abdicate in favor of his son Li Chun. Wang Shuwen was killed, and the eight sima were exiled. The reforms lasted only 146 days before failing.

v.t: Vikings discover Iceland
v.s: In 805, the Norwegian Viking navigator Naddodd was blown off course while sailing to the Faroe Islands and accidentally discovered the east coast of Iceland. He named the island 'Snæland' (Snowland). Later, another Viking, Garðar Svavarsson, circumnavigated Iceland, confirming it as an island. This discovery began the Norse colonization of Iceland.

v.t: Border conflicts between Byzantium and the Arabs in Anatolia
v.s: In the summer of 805, Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I broke the peace treaty with the Arabs and raided the Arab fortress of Sassoun. The Arab governor retaliated, capturing the Byzantine region of Galatia. Both sides fought indecisively in Asia Minor until a truce was reached in 806.

v.t: The Ghana Empire controls West African gold trade
v.s: In 805, the king of the Ghana Empire (Ghana meaning 'war lord') tightened control over the southern gold-producing regions, decreeing that all gold nuggets be submitted to the treasury and only gold dust be used among the people. The gold trade brought enormous tax revenue to Ghana, and its capital Kumbi Saleh became the terminal of trans-Saharan trade routes, where camel caravans brought salt and textiles in exchange for gold.

v.t: Emperor Xianzong ascends, determined to curtail frontier governors
v.s: In 806, Emperor Xianzong Li Chun ascended the throne, adopting the era name Yuanhe. Witnessing the eunuch installation and removal of his predecessor and the problem of powerful governors, he resolved to restore central authority. Xianzong appointed hawkish chancellors like Du Huangchang and Li Jifu, rejecting hereditary claims from the jiedushi of Chengde and Lulong, beginning the fifteen-year 'Yuanhe Restoration' campaign.

v.t: Liu Pi, jiedushi of Xichuan, rebels
v.s: In 806, Liu Pi, jiedushi of Xichuan, demanded control of Shannan West Road; when refused, he marched on Zizhou, openly rebelling. Emperor Xianzong ordered the general Gao Chongwen to lead 5,000 imperial guards to suppress the revolt. In the ninth month of the same year, the government forces recaptured Chengdu, and Liu Pi was captured and executed. This swift victory greatly intimidated other governors and restored some court prestige.

v.t: Emperor Saga of Japan ascends, implements new policies
v.s: In 806, after the death of Emperor Kammu, Emperor Saga ascended the throne. He abolished redundant offices, streamlined the bureaucracy, and established the 'Kurōdo-dokoro' (Chamber of Secretaries) as the emperor's private secretariat to counter the Fujiwara clan's regency. Emperor Saga was also a calligrapher, creating the 'Three Brushes of Saga' style, promoting the development of a distinct Japanese national culture.

v.t: Succession arrangements for the Frankish Empire
v.s: In 806, Charlemagne announced the 'Divisio Regnorum', splitting the empire into three parts: eldest son Charles would inherit the core Frankish region, second son Pepin received Italy, and youngest son Louis received Aquitaine and Burgundy. This arrangement aimed to avoid fraternal war after his death, but both Charles and Pepin predeceased their father, leaving Louis as sole heir to the entire empire.

v.t: Li Jifu compiles the 'Yuanhe State Accounting'
v.s: In 807, Chancellor Li Jifu compiled the 'Yuanhe State Accounting', detailing household numbers, tax quotas, and troop levels in each prefecture. The book showed that of the 48 circuits in the empire, only 15 were under direct court control; of the total 2.4 million households nationwide, 1.44 million paid no taxes to the court. The work revealed the fiscal crisis of the Tang and provided a basis for Xianzong's campaigns.

v.t: Death of Caliph Harun al-Rashid of the Arab Empire
v.s: In 807, the most famous Abbasid caliph, Harun al-Rashid, died of illness in Tus while on campaign in Khorasan. His reign (786-807) was a cultural peak of the Arab Empire, with Baghdad as a world center of learning. After his death, his sons al-Amin and al-Ma'mun fought a civil war over succession, plunging the empire into a decade of internal strife.

v.t: Second Council of Nicaea restores icon veneration
v.s: The Second Council of Nicaea in 787 had restored icon veneration under Empress Irene, but Charlemagne and the Western church rejected it. In 807, Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I reaffirmed the council's decisions and sent envoys to the pope to explain Eastern doctrine, seeking to heal the East-West schism, but without success.

v.t: Khwarizmi mathematician al-Khwarizmi teaches in Baghdad
v.s: In 807, the Persian mathematician Muhammad al-Khwarizmi entered the House of Wisdom in Baghdad as an astronomer and translator. He later wrote 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing' (algebra) and 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals', introducing Indian numerals (Arabic numerals) and algebra to the Islamic world. 'Algebra' derives from the Arabic 'al-jabr' (completion), and his Latinized name became a mathematical term.

v.t: Start of the Niu-Li factional strife
v.s: In the 808 imperial examination, candidates Niu Sengru and Li Zongmin criticized government policies. Chancellor Li Jifu (father of Li Deyu) slandered them to Xianzong, leading to their demotion. Over the next forty years, the Niu faction (led by Niu Sengru) and the Li faction (led by Li Deyu) engaged in bitter infighting, becoming a chronic political disease of the late Tang.

v.t: Charlemagne revises Saxon law
v.s: In 808, Charlemagne promulgated a new version of the 'Lex Saxonum', codifying Saxon customary law over two years. The new law abolished the blood feud system, replacing it with monetary compensation; it also forced Saxons to accept Christianity on pain of death. This intensified Saxon resistance but accelerated Christianization of the region.

v.t: Outbreak of the 'White Garment' movement in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 808, the 'White Garment' movement broke out in Khorasan against the Abbasid caliphate. Its leader, Hashim ibn Hakim, called himself the 'Veiled Prophet', preaching Mazdakite ideas of equality, attracting many peasants and slaves. The movement lasted until 810 and was eventually suppressed by forces sent by al-Ma'mun.

v.t: Yao rebellion on the Hunan-Guangxi border
v.s: In 808, the Yao people in the border area of Hunan and Guangxi, led by Pan Chong, revolted because local officials forced them to work as miners. They captured Daozhou, Jianghua, and other places. Emperor Xianzong dispatched troops to suppress the revolt, which lasted two years, though the local authorities were eventually compelled to reduce taxes.

v.t: Xianzong campaigns against Wang Chengzong of Chengde
v.s: In 809, Wang Shizhen, jiedushi of Chengde, died and his son Wang Chengzong declared himself acting military governor, refusing the court's appointment of a new governor. Emperor Xianzong ordered a campaign, with eunuch Tu Tuchengyi as commander, sending 200,000 troops to besiege Chengde. However, the government forces made little progress, and other governors supported Wang Chengzong. Eventually the court compromised and recognized Wang Chengzong. The campaign exposed the low combat effectiveness of the imperial guards and the evils of eunuch supervision.

v.t: Byzantium issues the new gold nomisma
v.s: In 809, Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I reformed the coinage, issuing a pure gold coin called the nomisma with lower gold content to ease the fiscal crisis caused by constant war. The new coin weighed a quarter less than the solidus of Justinian's time, but because of its stable purity and good reputation, it became an international settlement currency for Mediterranean trade, used for seven centuries.

v.t: Al-Ma'mun launches civil war for succession in the Arab Empire
v.s: On his deathbed in 809, Caliph Harun al-Rashid named his eldest son al-Amin as heir, with his second son al-Ma'mun as second in line. After al-Amin ascended, he attempted to exclude al-Ma'mun, who raised an army in Khorasan, sparking a four-year civil war. Al-Ma'mun gained Persian support, while al-Amin held Baghdad and Syria.

v.t: Saichō establishes Japanese Tendai sect
v.s: In 809, Saichō, returning from Tang China, officially founded the Japanese Tendai sect at Enryaku-ji on Mount Hiei. He integrated Tiantai Lotus Sutra teachings with esoteric Buddhism, Zen, and precepts, forming a comprehensive doctrine. Saichō also established the Mahayana Perfect Enlightenment precept platform, rivaling the Nara Buddhist sects. Mount Hiei thereafter became a sacred site of Japanese Buddhism.

v.t: Tang fiscal crisis intensifies
v.s: In 810, due to continuous military campaigns and revenue withheld by governors, the central treasury had only enough grain for three months. Xianzong adopted Li Jiang's suggestion, ordering a surtax on all prefectures and strictly limiting governors from intercepting the two taxes (household and land). This temporarily eased fiscal pressure but increased the burden on peasants, sparking many local uprisings.

v.t: Liu Zongyuan writes 'On Heaven'
v.s: In 810, Liu Zongyuan, exiled to Yongzhou as Sima, wrote the philosophical essay 'Tian Shuo' (On Heaven), refuting Han Yu's doctrine of a purposeful heaven. He argued that everything in the universe is composed of qi (vital energy) and that natural phenomena are unrelated to human fortune, advocating a materialist view: 'Merit is self-made, disaster self-caused'. The essay is an important representative of Tang atheism.

v.t: Decline of the Classic Maya city of Copán
v.s: In 810, Yax Pasaj, the last recorded king of Copán, died in war. Afterward, no more stelae were erected, and the population declined sharply. Archaeological evidence indicates prolonged drought, soil exhaustion, and elite infighting caused the gradual collapse of Classic Maya civilization, with inter-city trade networks breaking down.

v.t: Al-Ma'mun captures Baghdad and kills al-Amin
v.s: In 810, the civil war in the Arab Empire entered its decisive phase. Al-Ma'mun's Persian army besieged Baghdad for a year. Al-Amin attempted to break out but was captured and executed. Al-Ma'mun became the sole caliph, but he remained in Merv until 819 before returning to Baghdad. The House of Wisdom was damaged during the civil war, and many books were destroyed.

v.t: Bulgar Khan Krum crushes Byzantium
v.s: In July 811, Byzantine Emperor Nikephoros I led a large army into Bulgaria, burning the capital Pliska. However, Khan Krum ambushed the Byzantine army on its return at the Battle of Pliska (Varbitsa Pass). The Byzantine army was annihilated, and Nikephoros I was killed; Krum made his skull into a drinking cup. This defeat severely weakened Byzantium, forcing it to cede border lands and pay annual tribute of gold.

v.t: Emperor Xianzong promotes Li Jiang to chancellor
v.s: In 811, Xianzong appointed Li Jiang, who advocated a hard line against the jiedushi, as chancellor. Li Jiang advised tackling easier targets first, concentrating forces to eliminate Wu Yuanji of Huaixi while temporarily leaving the powerful northern circuits alone. He also dissuaded Xianzong from pursuing alchemy and urged him to be close to worthy ministers and distant from eunuchs. Li Jiang's support allowed the Yuanhe Restoration to continue.

v.t: Charlemagne issues the 'Imperial Testament', Louis the Pious succeeds
v.s: In 811, Charlemagne issued the 'Imperial Testament', confirming Louis as sole heir and having him crowned. Louis then convened a church council at Aachen to promote monastic reforms.

v.t: Kūkai establishes Japan's first school for commoners
v.s: In 811, Kūkai founded the 'Shugei Shuchi-in' at Tō-ji in Kyoto, admitting commoner children and teaching Confucianism, Buddhism, and medicine. This was Japan's first school open to the common people, breaking the aristocratic monopoly on education. Kūkai also compiled textbooks such as 'The Meaning of Sound and Word as Reality', emphasizing that language itself is the manifestation of the Dharma.

v.t: Tian Hongzheng of Weibo submits to the court
v.s: In 812, when the jiedushi of Weibo, Tian Ji'an, died, the army supported his uncle Tian Hongzheng. Breaking the tradition of Weibo's independence, Tian Hongzheng voluntarily submitted the census and maps of his six prefectures to the court, requesting that the court appoint a new military governor. Xianzong was overjoyed, appointing Tian Hongzheng as jiedushi and granting him a new name. Tian's submission marked a major breakthrough in the court's policy toward the Hebei governors.

v.t: Tang compiles the 'Yuanhe Clan Compendium'
v.s: In 812, court academician Lin Bao, commissioned by Xianzong, compiled the 'Yuanhe Xingzuan' in ten volumes, recording 1,232 surnames known in Tang, each with its commandery, founding ancestor, and notable Tang figures. It is an important source for the study of Tang aristocratic clans and population movements. The original is lost; the current text is a Qing reconstruction.

v.t: Vikings settle the Faroe Islands
v.s: Around 812, the Viking sailor Grímur Kamban led his family from Norway to the Faroe Islands, establishing a permanent settlement. The Faroe Islands have a mild climate suitable for sheep herding. The Norse language and culture influenced local society, and the islands became a staging post for Viking exploration of Iceland and Greenland.

v.t: Al-Ma'mun moves the Abbasid capital back to Baghdad
v.s: In 812, al-Ma'mun, after defeating al-Amin, decided to move the capital from Merv back to Baghdad due to pressure from Persian nobles. To stabilize his rule, he declared the Mu'tazili rationalist doctrine as state creed, suppressing traditionalist scholars. This sparked riots in Baghdad, and al-Ma'mun could not enter the city for two years.

v.t: Li Jifu compiles the 'Yuanhe Maps and Records of Prefectures and Counties'
v.s: In 813, Chancellor Li Jifu completed the 'Yuanhe Junxian Tuzhi' in 40 volumes plus 2 of contents. Using 813 as the cutoff, the work records the history, households, mountains, rivers, products, tribute, and military garrisons of each prefecture and county under the ten circuits, accompanied by maps. It is the earliest extant comprehensive geography of China; the maps are lost, but the text survives, providing valuable Tang geographical data.

v.t: Al-Ma'mun pacifies the civil war and reunifies the Arab Empire
v.s: In 813, al-Ma'mun's forces completely defeated the remnants of al-Amin, capturing Basra and Kufa. Ali al-Rida (the eighth Imam) publicly supported al-Ma'mun, gaining him Shia support. Al-Ma'mun pardoned the rebel commanders but executed al-Amin's close associates. The empire was reunified, but regional governors such as Tahir ibn Husayn gained great autonomy, sowing seeds of future fragmentation.

v.t: Bulgarian Khan Krum besieges Constantinople
v.s: In 813, Khan Krum of Bulgaria, taking advantage of the accession of the new Byzantine Emperor Leo V, laid siege to Constantinople with a large army. Krum built siege engines outside the walls, but the Byzantines held out using Greek fire and the strong fortifications. When winter came, Krum was forced to retreat and died en route. Byzantium gained a reprieve, but Thrace was occupied by Bulgaria.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'New Compilation of Surnames'
v.s: In 813, Emperor Saga commissioned the compilation of the 'Shinsen Shōjiroku' in thirty volumes, recording over a thousand Japanese surnames of imperial, divine, and foreign origin, tracing their roots. The work aimed to organize the clan system and establish the orthodoxy of the imperial line, and it is valuable for the study of ancient Japanese clan composition.

v.t: Death of Charlemagne the Great
v.s: On 28 January 814, Charlemagne died of pleurisy at his palace in Aachen at the age of 72. He had conquered Germania, Saxony, and Lombardy, and was crowned 'Emperor of the Romans' by the pope, establishing the political map of Western Christendom. After his death, his son Louis the Pious inherited the empire but failed to prevent its eventual division. Charlemagne's body was buried in a golden sarcophagus in Aachen Cathedral.

v.t: Wu Yuanji of Huaixi rebels
v.s: In 814, when Wu Shaoyang, jiedushi of Huaixi, died, his son Wu Yuanji concealed the death, took command of the army, and raided the surrounding area. Wu Yuanji controlled Shen, Guang, and Cai prefectures, directly threatening the eastern capital Luoyang. Emperor Xianzong, overriding opposition, ordered sixteen circuits to attack Huaixi. Due to the difficult terrain, government forces repeatedly failed, and the war lasted three years, becoming the hardest campaign of the Yuanhe Restoration.

v.t: Three-field crop rotation spreads in Europe
v.s: Around 814, the three-field system was widely adopted in the western Frankish countryside. Farmers divided their land into spring planting, autumn planting, and fallow sections, planting oats and barley in spring, wheat and rye in autumn. The system increased land use efficiency, raising grain yields by a third compared to the two-field system, promoting population recovery and the economic takeoff of the High Middle Ages.

v.t: Last king of Maya Tikal ascends
v.s: In 814, the last dated stela at Tikal mentions a king named 'Dark Sun'. No further stelae were erected afterward, indicating the city's decline. Archaeology shows that the population dropped sharply, palaces were abandoned, and the jungle gradually engulfed the buildings.

v.t: Assassination of Chancellor Wu Yuanheng
v.s: In June 815, Chancellor Wu Yuanheng was assassinated by arrows in the dark while on his way to court. At the same time, Censor-in-Chief Pei Du was seriously injured in another attack. The assassins were sent by the Ziqing jiedushi Li Shidao and Wang Chengzong. An enraged Xianzong ordered a search, eventually capturing dozens of assassins, who were executed. Wu's death severely damaged the hawkish faction at court, but Pei Du continued to direct the campaign against Huaixi after recovering.

v.t: Bai Juyi is demoted to Sima of Jiangzhou
v.s: In June 815, after the assassination of Chancellor Wu Yuanheng, Bai Juyi, a low-ranking official, submitted a memorial urging the arrest of the assassins. However, eunuchs and powerful ministers accused him of overstepping his position and of 'filial impiety' (his mother had died by falling into a well while viewing flowers, and Bai had written poems on flowers and wells). He was demoted to Sima of Jiangzhou. While there, Bai wrote the famous 'Song of the Pipa', lamenting 'outcasts from the capital' and turned to self-cultivation and Buddhist studies.

v.t: Al-Ma'mun establishes Mu'tazilism as state doctrine
v.s: In 815, Caliph al-Ma'mun publicly declared the Mu'tazili doctrine of the 'createdness of the Qur'an' as official, i.e., that the Qur'an was created by God rather than being eternal. He set up the Inquisition (mihna), forcing judges and scholars to accept this view. This provoked strong Sunni opposition, causing deep divisions in the Muslim community until it was abolished after al-Ma'mun's death.

v.t: Louis the Pious crowned Frankish emperor
v.s: In 815, Louis the Pious was crowned 'Augustus Emperor' by Pope Stephen V in Aachen Cathedral. Louis was Charlemagne's only surviving son, inheriting the entire empire, but his gentle, religious nature lacked his father's military talent. He attempted to divide the empire among his three sons, laying the groundwork for future civil wars.

v.t: Xianzong mobilizes sixteen circuits to besiege Huaixi
v.s: In 816, Xianzong appointed Han Hong, jiedushi of Xuanwu, as commander of the Huaixi campaign, mobilizing over 100,000 troops from sixteen circuits to encircle Wu Yuanji. But the jiedushi preserved their own strength and delayed action, leading to a prolonged stalemate and huge grain consumption. Xianzong was forced to replace the commander, appointing Li Su as jiedushi of Tang and Deng to lead the western offensive.

v.t: Pope Stephen V ascends
v.s: In 816, Pope Leo III died, and Stephen V was elected pope. A Roman noble, Stephen immediately allied with Frankish emperor Louis the Pious, recognizing Louis's protection of the papacy and obtaining a Frankish garrison in Italy to defend the Papal States, strengthening Frankish influence in Italy.

v.t: Polynesian chieftainships established in Samoa
v.s: Around 816, Polynesian navigators established complex social hierarchies in the Samoan Islands. They built large stone platforms for rituals and feasts. Samoa became the cradle of Polynesian culture, deeply influencing the later Tongan Empire.

v.t: Arab astronomer al-Khwarizmi completes the 'Sindhind Zij'
v.s: In 816, al-Khwarizmi completed the 'Sindhind Zij' (Astronomical Tables of the Sindhind) at the House of Wisdom. This work introduced Indian sine functions and decimal place-value arithmetic, improving on Ptolemy's models. Its Latin translation, 'Algoritmi de numero Indorum', later spread to Europe and promoted the revival of European astronomy.

v.t: Li Su's snow-night attack on Caizhou captures Wu Yuanji
v.s: On 15 October 817, under heavy snow, Li Su led 9,000 elite troops on a 130-li forced march straight to the Huaixi headquarters at Caizhou. The government forces broke through the outer and inner walls to the city, capturing Wu Yuanji in his sleep. He was sent to Chang'an and executed. The three prefectures of Huaixi were divided into three circuits, with court-appointed jiedushi. Li Su's snow-night attack became a classic example of surprise attack in ancient military history.

v.t: 'Pre-Verdun': Louis the Pious partitions the Frankish Empire among his sons
v.s: In 817, Louis the Pious convened an imperial council at Aachen and issued the 'Ordinatio Imperii', stipulating that his eldest son Lothair would be co-emperor and inherit the core of the empire, his second son Pepin would receive Aquitaine, and his third son Louis the German would receive Bavaria. However, Louis later excluded his youngest son Charles the Bald from the division, leading to several father-son civil wars and eventually the empire's division.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Kōnin Formula'
v.s: In 817, Emperor Saga ordered the compilation of the 'Kōnin Kyaku-shiki' in ten volumes, supplementing the Taihō and Yōrō Codes. 'Kyaku' refers to imperial edicts amending the codes, and 'shiki' to procedural rules for government offices. This work marked the beginning of Japan's 'formula' legal system, regulating official evaluations, land tax collection, and legal procedures.

v.t: Renewal of Byzantine iconoclasm
v.s: In 817, Byzantine Emperor Leo V, with the support of the Patriarch of Constantinople, revived iconoclasm, ordering the removal of icons and mosaics from churches and replacing them with crosses or geometric patterns. This second iconoclastic period lasted until 842. Leo V believed that icon veneration had led to military defeats. The policy was popular with the army but opposed by monks and the populace.

v.t: Xianzong pacifies Li Shidao of Ziqing
v.s: In 818, after pacifying Huaixi, Xianzong concentrated all national forces against Li Shidao, jiedushi of Ziqing, who refused to submit. The Xuanwu, Weibo, and Yicheng circuits attacked from four sides. Li Shidao's subordinate Liu Wu turned against him, captured Yunzhou, and killed Li Shidao. Ziqing was divided into three circuits. By this point, Xianzong had largely completed his reduction of the jiedushi, and the Yuanhe Restoration reached its peak.

v.t: Peak of the Translation Movement at the Abbasid House of Wisdom
v.s: In 818, Caliph al-Ma'mun personally oversaw the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, generously employing the translator Hunayn ibn Ishaq to translate Greek philosophical and medical works into Arabic. That year, translations of Aristotle's 'Metaphysics', Ptolemy's 'Geography', and Hippocrates' 'Aphorisms' were completed. These translations profoundly influenced Islamic philosophy and European scholasticism.

v.t: Omurtag succeeds as Bulgarian Khan
v.s: In 818, Omurtag, son of Khan Krum, became khan of Bulgaria. He concluded a thirty-year peace treaty with Byzantium, stabilizing the southern border, and then turned west to conquer Slavic tribes. Omurtag built palaces, cathedrals, and waterworks in the capital Pliska, ushering in a relatively prosperous period for Bulgaria.

v.t: Buddhist monk Huiguo dies at Qinglong Temple in Chang'an
v.s: In 818, Huiguo, the master of Kūkai and the seventh patriarch of Tang Esoteric Buddhism, died at Qinglong Temple. Huiguo was a direct disciple of Amoghavajra, mastering both the Womb and Diamond realms. He entrusted the lineage to Kūkai, prophesying that 'esoteric Buddhism will flourish greatly in Japan'. His Eastern Pagoda became the center of Tang Esoteric Buddhism, and his works remain essential texts for the Shingon sect.

v.t: Height of the Yuanhe Restoration under Emperor Xianzong
v.s: In 819, Xianzong completed the reduction of the Pinglu and Heng-Hai circuits that had not submitted. Except for the three Hebei circuits, all jiedushi now obeyed the court. Xianzong used this opportunity to reorganize officialdom, dismiss redundant officials, and reduce taxes, leading to agricultural recovery in the north. However, in his later years, Xianzong came to trust alchemists and took elixirs, making him irritable and setting the stage for his assassination by eunuchs.

v.t: Han Yu remonstrates against the Buddha relic and is exiled to Chaozhou
v.s: In the first month of 819, Xianzong ordered the Buddha relic from Famen Temple to be brought to the palace for three days of veneration, sparking a frenzy in Chang'an. Han Yu, Vice Minister of Justice, submitted a memorial ('On the Bone of Buddha') criticizing the emperor's devotion to Buddhism, urging that the relic be thrown into water or fire. An enraged Xianzong wanted to execute Han Yu, but after Pei Du and others pleaded for him, he was demoted to prefect of Chaozhou. While there, Han Yu promoted education and eliminated crocodiles, writing the famous lines: 'Clouds block the Qinling Mountains; my home is lost. Snow blocks the Blue Pass; my horse cannot move forward.'

v.t: Al-Ma'mun moves the Abbasid capital to Baghdad
v.s: In 819, al-Ma'mun ordered large-scale reconstruction of Baghdad, repairing walls, palaces, and bridges damaged during the civil war. He expanded the House of Wisdom, adding an observatory and library. Al-Ma'mun appointed non-Arabs (Persians) to high office, arousing Arab discontent, but this fostered a revival of Persian culture and the fusion of Islamic civilization.

v.t: Harald Fairhair of Norway begins unification wars
v.s: In 819, Harald Hårfagre (Harald Fairhair), a petty king in southeastern Norway, resolved to unify Norway. By marrying into the kingdom of Hordaland, he began conquering neighboring chieftains. Harald vowed not to cut or comb his hair until Norway was unified. The unification wars lasted more than a decade, laying the foundation for the Norwegian kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Xianzong of Tang (Li Chun, Jan) → Emperor Muzong of Tang (Li Heng)

v.t: Emperor Xianzong dies suddenly; Muzong ascends
v.s: On 27 January 820, Emperor Xianzong died suddenly in the Hall of Central Harmony of the Daming Palace after taking alchemical elixirs. Eunuch Chen Hongzhi, to conceal the assassination, enthroned the crown prince Li Heng as Emperor Muzong. Muzong indulged in pleasure, dismissed the hawkish chancellor Pei Du, and adopted an appeasement policy toward the Hebei circuits, causing the gains of the Yuanhe Restoration to be quickly lost.

v.t: Tibetan Emperor Ralpacan expands into the Hexi Corridor
v.s: In 820, taking advantage of Tang weakness, Tibetan Emperor Ralpacan sent troops to occupy Guazhou and Shazhou in the Hexi Corridor, cutting the Tang's route to the Western Regions. In Dunhuang, the Tibetans enforced Tibetan customs (forcing Chinese to adopt Tibetan dress and hairstyle) while supporting Buddhism; several Tibetan-style caves were carved in the Mogao Caves during this period.

v.t: Foundation of Kievan Rus'
v.s: According to the 'Primary Chronicle', in 820 the Varangian chieftain Rurik established the first Rus' dynasty, the Rurikids, in Novgorod. He and his Norse retinue conquered Slavic tribes, collecting tribute and forming a state. His successor Oleg later moved south, captured Kiev, and made it the capital, hence 'Kievan Rus'.

v.t: Tang jiedushi rebel again; the Hebei circuits are lost
v.s: After Muzong's accession in 820, he implemented a 'demobilization' policy, disbanding some troops from the jiedushi. The unemployed soldiers caused instability in Hebei. A mutiny in Lulong killed its jiedushi, and a similar event in Chengde killed Tian Hongzheng. Muzong was forced to recognize the mutineers as jiedushi; the three Hebei circuits returned to de facto independence, and the Tang would never again be able to subdue them.

v.t: Vikings invade Ireland, found Dublin
v.s: In 820, Viking pirates landed on the east coast of Ireland, plundering monasteries and villages. They established a permanent camp at the mouth of the River Liffey, which later became Dublin. The Vikings used Dublin as a base to control Irish Sea trade, intermarrying with the native Gaels and creating a Hiberno-Norse culture.

v.t: Longqing Alliance: Tang and Tibet establish boundary monument
v.s: In 821, the Tang and Tibet held a treaty ceremony in Chang'an, agreeing to a boundary along the Yellow River and the Helan Mountains, with mutual non-aggression. A stele was erected in Lhasa, inscribed in both Chinese and Tibetan: 'The states become one, establishing a great peace treaty, never to be broken'. The stele still stands before the Jokhang Temple, a historical witness to Sino-Tibetan friendship.

v.t: Mutiny in Youzhou; jiedushi Zhang Hongjing imprisoned
v.s: In July 821, soldiers of the Lulong circuit mutinied over reduced rewards, seized Zhang Hongjing, and installed Zhu Kerong as acting governor. Muzong, instead of attacking, recognized Zhu's position. The Hebei jiedushi became independent again, and the court lost all authority.

v.t: Emperor Muzong neglects government, indulges in extravagance
v.s: In 821, Emperor Muzong ignored state affairs, spending his days on polo, hunting, and entertainment. He undertook grand construction projects, gave lavish rewards to eunuchs and performers, and increased salt and tea taxes to cover the costs of the campaigns. Factional strife between the Niu and Li parties worsened, and governance deteriorated.

v.t: Formation of the East Frankish kingdom
v.s: In 821, Louis the German, son of Louis the Pious, was granted Bavaria and the eastern marches. He established his court at Regensburg, recruited Slavic mercenaries, and built a loyal army. He encouraged the spread of Benedictine monasteries eastward, promoting Christianization of the eastern border, laying the foundation for an independent East Francia (Germany).

v.t: The three Hebei circuits rebel again, permanent independence of jiedushi
v.s: In 822, a mutiny in Chengde killed jiedushi Tian Hongzheng, and the army installed Wang Tingcou; Weibo expelled its jiedushi and installed Shi Xiancheng. Muzong ordered Pei Du to attack, but the government forces ran out of supplies and the soldiers were weary. Eventually, the court recognized Wang Tingcou and Shi Xiancheng as jiedushi. The three Hebei circuits remained independent for the next 150 years.

v.t: Large-scale astronomical observations in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 822, under Caliph al-Ma'mun's orders, Arab astronomers established two observatories, one at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad and one in Damascus, to simultaneously observe the sun, moon, and planets. They measured the obliquity of the ecliptic as 23°33', close to the modern value, and calculated the length of a degree of meridian as 111.8 km, with very little error. These results were compiled in the 'Damascus Tables'.

v.t: Viking colonization of Normandy
v.s: In 822, the Danish Viking chieftain Hastein led a fleet into the Seine estuary and established a settlement at Rouen. In exchange for tribute to Frankish king Charles the Bald, they obtained land and gradually assimilated, adopting Christianity. These Vikings came to be known as Normans, and the Duchy of Normandy they founded later became the base for the conquest of England.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Ryōunshū' anthology of Chinese poetry
v.s: In 822, Emperor Saga commissioned the compilation of the 'Ryōunshū' in one volume, containing 91 Chinese poems by 24 poets, including the emperor himself. This was Japan's first imperially commissioned anthology of Chinese verse, marking a shift from imitation to independent composition, with a refined style incorporating Japanese themes.

v.t: Kūkai establishes Shingon sect in Japan
v.s: In 823, Kūkai was granted Tō-ji in Kyoto as a main temple and officially founded the Japanese Shingon sect (Eastern Esoteric Buddhism). He wrote works such as 'The Ten Stages of the Mind' and 'Treatise on the Two Teachings', systematically expounding the Shingon doctrine of 'attaining Buddhahood in this very body' through the three mysteries of mantra, mudra, and visualization. Shingon profoundly influenced Japanese samurai culture, tea ceremony, and other arts.

v.t: Emperor Muzong suffers a stroke; crown prince acts as regent
v.s: In 823, due to long-term consumption of elixirs and overindulgence, Emperor Muzong had a stroke and could no longer govern. Crown Prince Li Zhan (later Emperor Jingzong) began to act as regent. Eunuch Wang Shoucheng took advantage to monopolize power, marginalizing chancellor Pei Du, and the government grew increasingly corrupt.

v.t: Louis the Pious imprisoned by his sons
v.s: In 823, Louis the Pious, favoring his youngest son Charles the Bald, tried to deprive Lothair of his imperial inheritance, causing his three elder sons to rebel. Lothair marched on Aachen, imprisoned his father in a monastery, and forced him to confess his sins publicly. This civil war exposed the Carolingian dynasty's tendency to fragmentation.

v.t: Nanzhao sends envoys to pay tribute to Tang
v.s: In 823, King Quan Lisheng of Nanzhao sent a 300-man mission with elephants and gold-silver ware to Chang'an to improve relations. The Tang court reciprocated with brocade robes and silver belts, and awarded the title 'Prince of Nanzhao'. Nanzhao would later alternately attack and submit, becoming a long-term threat to Tang's southwestern border.

v.t: Emperor Muzong dies; Jingzong ascends
v.s: In the first month of 824, Emperor Muzong died at age 30. Crown Prince Li Zhan ascended as Emperor Jingzong, only 15. Jingzong was even more dissolute than his father, obsessed with polo and hunting, often spending nights out of the palace. Eunuchs Li Fengji and Wang Shoucheng took turns in power, and the government became even more corrupt.

v.t: Han Yu dies
v.s: In the twelfth month of 824, Han Yu, the leader of the Tang classical prose movement, died at his home in Chang'an at age 57. Han Yu opposed the parallel prose style and advocated that literature should convey the Way. His works such as 'On Teachers' and 'Explanation of Studies' have had a lasting influence. He also fought against Buddhism and Daoism, defending the Confucian orthodoxy, and is honored as the foremost of the 'Eight Prose Masters of the Tang-Song Era'. He was posthumously awarded the title of Minister of Rites and the epithet 'Wen' (Cultured).

v.t: Power struggle between pope and Frankish emperor
v.s: In 824, Pope Eugene II convened a council in Rome, reaffirming papal authority above the emperor. But the Frankish emperor Lothair I sent troops into Italy, forcing the pope to recognize Frankish suzerainty over the Papal States. A compromise was reached requiring imperial approval of papal elections, foreshadowing the Investiture Controversy.

v.t: University established in Córdoba by the Umayyads of Spain
v.s: In 824, Emir Abd al-Rahman II of Córdoba established a university (madrasa) next to the Great Mosque of Córdoba. It taught medicine, astronomy, philosophy, and Arabic grammar, attracting Christian and Jewish scholars who translated Greek works. Córdoba became a major European center of learning.

v.t: Emperor Jingzong neglects affairs, obsessed with polo
v.s: In 825, Emperor Jingzong ignored state affairs, spending his days organizing polo matches and wrestling shows, even hunting foxes at night. He often left the palace in disguise to hunt, causing panic among the people. He also launched a costly renovation of the Luoyang palaces, depleting the treasury. Remonstrance officials' advice was ignored, and eunuchs took the opportunity to frame loyal ministers, deepening government corruption.

v.t: Al-Khwarizmi completes 'The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing' (Algebra)
v.s: In 825, the Arab mathematician al-Khwarizmi wrote 'Al-Kitāb al-mukhtaṣar fī ḥisāb al-jabr wa-l-muqābala', systematically explaining the solution of six standard forms of quadratic equations, such as 'square equals root', 'square plus root equals number', and so on. He used algebraic symbols for 'root' and 'square'. The book was translated into Latin in the 12th century, and the word 'algebra' comes from the Arabic 'al-jabr' (restoration).

v.t: Expansion of Samye Monastery in Tibet
v.s: In 825, the Tibetan Emperor Ralpacan (Kelek Kezu) greatly expanded Samye Monastery, adding pagodas, halls, and monks' quarters. Samye became the first Tibetan monastery with the 'three jewels' (Buddha, Dharma, Sangha) complete, and its architecture blended Chinese, Indian, and Tibetan styles. Ralpacan instituted a system of seven families supporting one monk, bringing Buddhism to a peak in Tibet.

v.t: Tangut party moves east to Xiazhou
v.s: In 825, under pressure from Tibet, the Tangut chieftain Tuoba Sigong led his people from the northeastern Tibetan Plateau to Xiazhou (present-day Jingbian, Shaanxi) and attached themselves to the Tang. The Tang court established the Xia-Sui-Yin circuit, appointed Tuoba Sigong as its jiedushi, and granted him the surname Li. The Tanguts thus took control of Xiazhou, laying a foundation for the future Western Xia state.

v.t: Emperor Jingzong killed by eunuchs
v.s: On 8 December 826, after returning from hunting foxes at night, Emperor Jingzong was killed by the eunuch Liu Keming, aged 18. Liu issued a false edict enthroning Li Wu, Prince of Jiang. Other eunuchs, Wang Shoucheng and Liang Shouqian, led the Shence Army to kill Liu and Li Wu, and enthroned Jingzong's younger brother Li Ang as Emperor Wenzong. This coup demonstrated the eunuchs' military power, making emperors puppets.

v.t: Danish king Harald Klak baptized
v.s: In 826, the Danish king Harald Klak, seeking Frankish help against his rivals, was baptized in Mainz, becoming the first Danish king to accept Christianity. Louis the Pious granted him land and sent missionary Ansgar with him. Christianity spread slowly in Denmark thereafter, but Harald was later forced to tolerate polytheism and renounced his faith several times.

v.t: Egbert of Wessex conquers Kent
v.s: In 826, King Egbert of Wessex conquered the kingdom of Kent and forced East Anglia, Sussex, and other small kingdoms to submit. He cemented control through marriage, giving his daughter to a Kentish prince. Egbert's conquests laid the groundwork for the unification of England, and he is honored as the 'first king of the Anglo-Saxons'.

v.t: Babak uprising in Azerbaijan in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 826, the Khurramite movement broke out in Azerbaijan, led by Babak Khorramdin, who called for land redistribution and abolition of taxes. The rebels captured towns and controlled most of Azerbaijan and Armenia for over twenty years. The Arab Empire sent several punitive expeditions without success until 837.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong ascends and tries to revive government
v.s: In 827, 18-year-old Emperor Wenzong ascended. He diligently attended to affairs, recalled upright officials such as Li Deyu and Li Jiang, and demoted the eunuch Li Fengji. He dressed plainly, reduced palace ladies, and released hunting dogs and falcons, aiming to restore the dynasty's vigor. He resolved to eliminate eunuch power, but lacking military support, his efforts ultimately led to the tragic Ganlu Incident.

v.t: Arab army captures Byzantine Sicily
v.s: In June 827, the Aghlabid emir Ziyadat Allah I sent a fleet of over 100 ships to invade Sicily, landing at Marsala. The Byzantine defenders were ineffective, and the Arabs captured Palermo and other cities, establishing the Emirate of Sicily. The Byzantine navy attempted counterattacks but repeatedly failed, and Sicily gradually became Islamized.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Keikokushū' Chinese poetry anthology
v.s: In 827, Emperor Junna commissioned the compilation of the 'Keikokushū' in 20 volumes, containing over 200 Chinese poems by 118 poets. The title means 'governing the state and helping the people', and the poems reflect a pragmatic spirit, showing the political philosophy of early Heian intellectuals. Most of the original is lost, with only fragments surviving.

v.t: Ghana Empire takes control of Saharan trade routes
v.s: In 827, King Kousi of Ghana used his cavalry to take control of the trade routes from Sijilmasa to Awdaghost. He fixed the exchange rate between salt blocks and gold, collecting heavy taxes. The Ghana Empire became the most powerful kingdom in West Africa, with its capital Kumbi Saleh reaching 20,000 inhabitants.

v.t: Vikings launch large-scale invasion of England
v.s: In 828, a large Viking fleet raided the southeast coast of England, sacking Canterbury and London. King Egbert of Wessex fought back but suffered heavy losses. Thereafter, the Vikings raided each autumn and returned to Denmark each spring, marking a high point of the 'Viking Age' in England.

v.t: Death of Alcuin of York
v.s: In 828, Alcuin of York, the leading figure of the Carolingian Renaissance, died in Aachen. Alcuin had been abbot of Tours, reformed monastic education, compiled grammar and rhetoric textbooks, and revised the Latin Vulgate Bible. His disciples spread classical learning across European monasteries.

v.t: Angkor Dynasty expands capital Hariharalaya
v.s: In 828, King Jayavarman II of Angkor built an artificial reservoir, Indratataka, in the Roluos area and a temple on an island in its center. He designed the capital according to Indian cosmology, with a central mountain-temple housing a Shiva linga. This marked the beginning of the Angkorian urban planning model.

v.t: Nanzhao attacks Annam Protectorate
v.s: In 828, King Quan Lisheng of Nanzhao attacked the Tang Annam Protectorate (northern Vietnam), capturing the city of Jiaozhi (present-day Hanoi). The Tang court dispatched troops from Lingnan, and the Nanzhao forces retreated after looting. Nanzhao continued to harass the border, and the Tang were forced to reinforce the Lingnan defense line.

v.t: Nanzhao king Fengyou invades Chengdu and sacks the city
v.s: In 829, Nanzhao king Fengyou led his army to break through the outer city of Chengdu, looting for ten days before retreating. They captured tens of thousands of artisans, monks, and silk weavers, along with books and Buddhist statues. The advanced textile and building techniques of the Tang were thus brought to Yunnan, boosting local development. Meanwhile, the Tang's Xichuan region was devastated; Wenzong dismissed jiedushi Du Yuanying and appointed Li Deyu in his place.

v.t: Egbert unifies the Seven Kingdoms of England
v.s: In 829, King Egbert of Wessex defeated Mercia at the Battle of Ellendun and forced Northumbria to submit. He became the first king to be formally recognized as 'Bretwalda' (overlord of Britain), marking the end of the Heptarchy. However, his unification lasted only a decade, as renewed Viking invasions caused further fragmentation.

v.t: Japanese monk Ennin travels to Tang to study Buddhism
v.s: In 829, the Japanese Tendai monk Ennin accompanied an embassy to Tang China, studying in Yangzhou, Wutai Mountain, and Chang'an. He stayed for ten years and wrote the 'Record of a Pilgrimage to Tang in Search of the Dharma', which contains detailed accounts of Tang society and the Huichang persecution of Buddhism, making it a valuable source on Sino-Japanese exchange.

v.t: Arab astronomer al-Farghani completes 'Elements of Astronomy'
v.s: In 829, the Arab astronomer Ahmad al-Farghani (Alfraganus) wrote 'Elements of Astronomy', summarizing the Ptolemaic system and explaining planetary motion, day length, and calendar reckoning. Translated into Latin in the 12th century, it became a standard university astronomy textbook in Europe, cited by Copernicus and Dante.

v.t: Accelerated Islamization of the East African coast
v.s: In 830, Arab merchants established permanent settlements at Sofala, Mombasa, and Zanzibar, intermarrying with the local Bantu population to form Swahili culture. Islam spread rapidly among coastal towns, with rulers calling themselves sultans and using Arabic as a trade language. East Africa became integrated into the Indian Ocean trading network.

v.t: Indian numerals introduced to the Arab Empire
v.s: In 830, in his work 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals', al-Khwarizmi systematically introduced the Indian numerals (0-9) and the decimal place-value system. He demonstrated that using these figures made arithmetic far easier than with Roman numerals. The book was translated into Latin, and 'Arabic numerals' then entered Europe, revolutionizing mathematical calculation.

v.t: Second civil war among Louis the Pious and his sons
v.s: In 830, Emperor Louis the Pious attempted to deprive his sons Louis the German and Pepin of their lands, giving all to his youngest son Charles the Bald. The elder brothers again rebelled, captured Louis, and forced him to exile his wife Judith. Louis was freed the following year, but the empire's unity was already beyond repair.

v.t: Prelude to the rise of the Mali Empire in West Africa
v.s: Around 830, a small chiefdom called Mali, of the Mandinka people, rose in the upper Niger River region. The area was rich in gold and iron ore and controlled part of the salt trade. Mali expanded rapidly, paving the way for Sundiata's establishment of the Mali Empire in the 13th century.

v.t: Civil war in Tibet; Buddhist monks take power
v.s: In 831, Tibetan Emperor Ralpacan, due to excessive devotion to Buddhism, handed over military and political authority to a monk, triggering intense resentment among the nobility. Anti-Buddhist nobles assassinated Ralpacan and installed his younger brother Lang Darma as emperor. Lang Darma immediately launched an anti-Buddhist persecution, destroying monasteries and forcing monks to return to lay life, plunging Tibet into civil war and causing the empire's rapid collapse.

v.t: Arabs capture Malta
v.s: In 831, the Aghlabid navy captured Malta, destroying the Byzantine garrison and churches, and establishing a naval base. Malta served as a stepping stone between North Africa and Sicily and was ruled by Arabs for over two centuries, with the Arabic language and Islam profoundly influencing the island.

v.t: Magyars enter the Carpathian Basin
v.s: In 831, the nomadic Magyars (Hungarians), originally from the Ural Mountains, were pushed westward by the Khazars and Pechenegs into the Carpathian Basin. They formed a confederation of seven tribes and excelled at horse archery, beginning to raid Central Europe and Italy.

v.t: Tang chancellor Song Shenxi falsely accused by eunuchs and exiled
v.s: In 831, Emperor Wenzong secretly plotted with Chancellor Song Shenxi to eliminate the eunuchs. The eunuch Wang Shoucheng learned of the plan and preemptively accused Song of plotting rebellion. Wenzong was forced to demote Song to a minor post, and he died in exile. This event made Wenzong realize how powerful the eunuchs had become and forced him to seek other opportunities.

v.t: Wenzong secretly trains new troops to kill eunuchs
v.s: In 832, Wenzong decided to rely on one eunuch, Wang Shoucheng, against another, Qiu Shiliang. He secretly appointed Zheng Zhu as jiedushi of Fengxiang, where he recruited and trained soldiers in Fengtian (modern Qianxian) as a force to eliminate the eunuchs. But the plan was leaked, forcing Wenzong to act prematurely.

v.t: Arabs occupy Cyprus
v.s: In 832, an Arab fleet landed on Cyprus, forcing the Byzantine garrison to surrender. The two sides agreed that Cyprus would remain neutral, with tax revenues split equally. Thereafter, Arab and Byzantine forces co-existed on the island, which served as a buffer for nearly a century.

v.t: House of Wisdom in Baghdad expands into a major library
v.s: In 832, Caliph al-Ma'mun expanded the House of Wisdom into a comprehensive complex of library, academy, and translation center, housing 400,000 volumes. It employed hundreds of scribes, bookbinders, and translators, using high-quality paper from newly introduced Arab papermaking. Its collection included Greek, Persian, and Indian classics, making it the largest repository of knowledge in the world at the time.

v.t: Fujiwara regency begins to take shape in Japan
v.s: In 832, Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu's daughter became empress consort of Emperor Ninmyō, allowing the Fujiwara clan to intervene in affairs through marriage ties. Fujiwara no Fuyutsugu established the position of 'Sesshō' (regent), originally meant to assist underage emperors, but it later evolved into a permanent institution that sidelined the emperor.

v.t: Decline of the Langya Wang clan in Tang politics
v.s: In 833, Wang Ya, a chancellor from the Langya Wang clan, was dismissed by Wenzong after associating with Li Xun and Zheng Zhu. The Langya Wang, a top aristocratic lineage since the Wei-Jin period, was now politically marginalized, marking the decline of the old aristocracy and the rise of new officials from the examination system.

v.t: Arab astronomers complete translation of Ptolemy's 'Almagest'
v.s: In 833, the translation team led by Hunayn ibn Ishaq completed an Arabic version of Ptolemy's 'Almagest' (called 'Al-Majisṭī'), adding extensive commentary and corrected data. This work became the foundation of Arabic astronomy and profoundly influenced medieval European astronomy.

v.t: Lothair I crowned co-emperor of the Frankish Empire
v.s: In 833, under the mediation of Pope Gregory IV, Lothair I reconciled with his father Louis the Pious and was crowned co-emperor, receiving the eastern territories. But the fraternal animosity did not subside, and the Frankish empire existed in name only.

v.t: Li Deyu appointed jiedushi of Xichuan, strengthens border defenses
v.s: In 833, Li Deyu was appointed jiedushi of Xichuan, responsible for defending against Nanzhao and Tibet. He built fortresses such as Qionglai and Qingxi Pass, trained crossbowmen and cavalry, and sent agents to Nanzhao to spread Tang power. Li Deyu's border policies were effective, bringing temporary peace to the Xichuan frontier.

v.t: Vikings sack the major trading emporium of Dorestad
v.s: In 834, the Danish Viking chieftain Rorik led a fleet in a surprise attack on Dorestad (near present-day Utrecht), the Frankish Empire's most important trading port. They burned warehouses, extorted ransoms, and forced the Frankish king to pay danegeld. Subsequent annual Viking raids caused Dorestad's rapid decline, shifting northern European trade centers elsewhere.

v.t: Zheng Zhu and Li Xun become Wenzong's confidants
v.s: In 834, after a serious illness, Wenzong increasingly trusted the physician Zheng Zhu and the scripture lecturer Li Xun, who urged him to eliminate the eunuchs. Wenzong appointed Li Xun as Hanlin Academician and Zheng Zhu as Court Official, involving them in key decisions, preparing for the Ganlu Incident.

v.t: Death of Arab mathematician al-Khwarizmi
v.s: In 834, the father of algebra, al-Khwarizmi, died in Baghdad. Not only did he pioneer algebra, but he also introduced Indian numerals to the Arab world. His works 'Algebra' and 'Indian Calculation' had an immeasurable impact on the subsequent development of mathematics.

v.t: Indravarman I of Angkor constructs canals
v.s: In 834, King Indravarman I of Angkor began building a large canal several kilometers long, bringing water from the Siem Reap River to the capital. He also built the first large reservoir of Angkor, the Indratataka, for agricultural irrigation. The water management system laid the material foundation for Angkor's prosperity.

v.t: The Ganlu (Sweet Dew) Incident
v.s: On 21 November 835, Wenzong plotted with Li Xun and Zheng Zhu to eliminate the eunuchs. Li Xun spread a rumor that sweet dew had fallen on the cypress trees in the courtyard of the Jinwu Office, luring the eunuch leaders Qiu Shiliang and Yu Hongzhi to come see. The ambushed soldiers failed to kill Qiu, who realized the trap. Qiu returned to the palace, seized Wenzong, and closed the palace gates, launching a massacre. Li Xun, Zheng Zhu, and Chancellor Wang Ya were among more than a thousand killed. After the incident, eunuchs became even more arrogant, and Wenzong was effectively imprisoned until his death.

v.t: Chen Cangqi compiles 'Supplements to the Materia Medica'
v.s: In 835, the folk physician Chen Cangqi completed the 'Bencao Shiyi' in ten volumes, adding 692 folk medicinal herbs not included in the 'Newly Revised Materia Medica' and correcting some errors. He proposed the 'ten categories' of drugs, enriching Chinese pharmacology.

v.t: The Niu-Li factional strife intensifies
v.s: After the Ganlu Incident in 835, Li Xun and Zheng Zhu failed, and Li Deyu was demoted. The Niu faction, led by Niu Sengru and Yang Sifu, came to power and attacked Li faction members. The two factions attacked each other, placing private grudges above state interests, causing severe government corruption.

v.t: The circular military city of Samarra is built
v.s: In 835, Caliph al-Mu'tasim moved the capital from Baghdad to Samarra (meaning 'he who sees it rejoices') and built a huge circular military city. Samarra featured a spiral minaret (Malwiya) and vast palaces, housing Turkic slave soldiers who eventually gained real power in the Abbasid state.

v.t: The Uyghur Khaganate collapses; its people migrate westward
v.s: In 836 (some sources say 840), the Yenisei Kyrgyz, allied with rebellious Uyghur generals, captured the Uyghur capital Ordu-Baliq and killed the khagan. The Uyghurs split into three westward migrations: one moved to the Hexi Corridor, later forming the Ganzhou Uyghurs; another to Gaochang (Turfan), establishing the Qocho Uyghur kingdom; and a third moved west of the Pamirs, establishing the Kara-Khanid Khanate. The Uyghur migration profoundly affected the ethnic and religious landscape of Central Asia.

v.t: Death of Kūkai in Japan
v.s: In 836, Kūkai, the founder of Japanese Shingon Buddhism, died on Mount Kōya at age 62. A prolific writer, he had established the first private school open to commoners, the Shugei Shuchi-in, and created the hiragana syllabary based on Chinese cursive script. He was posthumously awarded the title 'Kōbō Daishi' (Great Master of the Propagation of the Dharma), and Shingon continues to have millions of followers.

v.t: Vikings establish the Kingdom of Limerick in Ireland
v.s: In 836, Vikings established a permanent base at the mouth of the Shannon River on the west coast of Ireland, which later became the city of Limerick. They ruled southwestern Ireland and competed with the Viking Kingdom of Dublin for dominance of the Irish Sea trade.

v.t: Threat from Nanzhao to Tang's Annam Protectorate diminishes
v.s: In 836, the Tang Annam Protectorate governor Han Yue repaired fortifications and trained local troops, successfully repelling small Nanzhao raids. With internal succession struggles in Nanzhao, they were temporarily unable to attack north, giving Annam more than a decade of peace.

v.t: Last Tibetan emperor Lang Darma persecutes Buddhism
v.s: In 837, Lang Darma officially implemented anti-Buddhist policies across Tibet. He closed major monasteries such as Samye, destroyed Buddhist images, burned scriptures, and forced monks to return to lay life or become soldiers. Many Indian and Chinese monks fled to Ladakh and Nepal. Lang Darma's persecution marked the first end of Buddhism in Tibet, which was not restored for over a century.

v.t: Arab army attacks Byzantine Anatolia
v.s: In 837, Caliph al-Mu'tasim sent Turkic slave soldiers to attack the Byzantine Anatolic theme, capturing the city of Ankara and enslaving many residents. Byzantine Emperor Theophilos was unable to counterattack and was forced to sue for peace, paying annual tribute to the Arabs.

v.t: First Rus' attack on Constantinople
v.s: In 837, the Rus' (Viking-descended Slavs) sailed across the Black Sea and appeared before the walls of Constantinople for the first time. The Byzantine navy repelled the Rus' fleet with Greek fire. This was the first recorded appearance of the Rus', who would later frequently attack or negotiate with Byzantium.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong suffers a mental breakdown, abandons government
v.s: In 837, after the Ganlu Incident, Wenzong fell into depression and mental confusion. He entrusted government to the eunuch Qiu Shiliang and spent his days drinking and praying to Buddha. He lamented, 'I am controlled by my servants, worse than King Nan of Zhou or Emperor Xian of Han!' The power of the eunuchs reached its peak.

v.t: Frankish empire effectively ends due to internal division
v.s: In 838, Louis the Pious's sons, Louis the German and Charles the Bald, united against Lothair I, who was forced to give up his imperial inheritance and retain only Italy. The three brothers signed a temporary agreement, each ruling his own kingdom; the Carolingian empire was in effect divided into three parts.

v.t: Japanese envoy Fujiwara no Tsunetsugu travels to Tang
v.s: In 838, the last large-scale Japanese envoy mission to Tang, led by Fujiwara no Tsunetsugu, set sail with four ships. Among the mission was the monk Ennin. After this, due to Tang internal unrest and maritime risks, Japan suspended official embassies for nearly sixty years.

v.t: Nanzhao attacks Annam, captures Jiaozhi
v.s: In 838, Nanzhao king Fengyou took advantage of the eunuch-dominated Tang court to capture Jiaozhi; the Annam protectorate governor Li Zhuo abandoned the city and fled. Nanzhao occupied Annam for four years until Gao Pian recaptured it.

v.t: Arab philosopher al-Kindi teaches in Baghdad
v.s: In 838, the Arab philosopher Abu Yusuf al-Kindi translated and commented on Aristotle's 'Metaphysics' at the House of Wisdom, attempting to reconcile Islamic theology with Greek philosophy. Known as the 'father of Arab philosophy', his works deeply influenced al-Farabi and Avicenna.

v.t: Córdoba under the Umayyads flourishes
v.s: In 839, Emir Abd al-Rahman II expanded the capital Córdoba into a metropolis with libraries, baths, and a university. He introduced luxury manufacturing techniques from the East and employed Arab, Jewish, and Christian scholars to translate classical works together. Córdoba became a bridge for cultural exchange between Europe and the Islamic world.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong becomes seriously ill; succession dispute
v.s: In 839, Wenzong's health deteriorated. He wanted to establish Chen Wang Chengmei as crown prince. The eunuchs Qiu Shiliang and Yu Hongzhi opposed him, detained the edict, and forcibly installed Wenzong's younger brother Li Yan as emperor. Wenzong died a few months later in grief.

v.t: Kilwa Sultanate mints silver coins
v.s: In 839, the Kilwa Sultanate began minting silver coins called 'Kilwa coins', bearing Arabic inscriptions. They were the first indigenous coins in East Africa, showing Kilwa's commercial prosperity and sovereign independence.

v.t: Emperor Wenzong dies; Emperor Wuzong ascends
v.s: In 839 (actually early 840), Emperor Wenzong died, and the eunuch Qiu Shiliang installed Li Yan as Emperor Wuzong. Wuzong was an energetic ruler who curbed eunuch power and appointed Li Deyu, initiating the Huichang Restoration.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong ascends, appoints Li Deyu
v.s: In the first month of 840, after ascending, Emperor Wuzong immediately dismissed Niu faction members and recalled Li Deyu as chancellor. Li Deyu proposed a three-point plan to control the jiedushi: streamline the army, strengthen the Jianghuai grain transport, and divide the surrendered Uyghurs. Wuzong fully trusted Li Deyu, and the two cooperated to create the most stable period of the late Tang – the Huichang Restoration.

v.t: Uyghurs migrate westward to the Hexi Corridor
v.s: In 840, after the destruction of the Uyghur Khaganate by the Kyrgyz, a branch led by a minister surrendered to the Tang and was settled in the Hexi Corridor. The Tang court granted its leader a military title. These Uyghurs later became part of the Ganzhou Uyghurs, helping to check the Tangut.

v.t: Louis the Pious dies; Frankish empire formally splits
v.s: In June 840, Emperor Louis the Pious died in Ingelheim at age 62. He failed to unify the empire before his death, and his three sons – Lothair, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald – immediately fell into civil war, leading to the Treaty of Verdun in 843 that formally divided the empire.

v.t: Samarra develops a lavish court culture
v.s: In 840, Samarra grew into a lavish capital with palaces, mosques, gardens, a zoo, and a racetrack. Caliph al-Mu'tasim relied on Turkic slave soldiers, who gradually gained real power, making the caliph a puppet and ushering in the 'Anarchy at Samarra' of the Abbasid dynasty.

v.t: Li Deyu becomes chancellor and implements reforms
v.s: In 841, Li Deyu formally became chancellor, holding the titles of head of the Chancellery and Deputy Head of the Secretariat. He centralized power, abolished redundant prefectural posts, reduced salary expenditures, and improved the grain transport to supply Chang'an. He also severely punished corrupt officials, decreeing that prefects would be held jointly responsible for crimes. This period was called the 'Huichang Governing Style'.

v.t: Viking establishment of the Duchy of Normandy begins
v.s: In 841, the Viking chieftain Rollo captured Rouen and advanced up the Seine. Charles the Bald, king of West Francia, unable to expel him, was forced to recognize Rollo as Count of Normandy on condition that he convert to Christianity and prevent other Viking incursions. This created the embryonic Duchy of Normandy.

v.t: Byzantine-Arab border truce
v.s: In 841, Byzantine Emperor Theophilos and Caliph al-Mu'tasim signed a ten-year truce on the Anatolian border. Byzantium agreed to pay annual tribute, and the Arabs recognized Byzantine control over Asia Minor. Both sides needed peace due to internal rebellions, and the truce lasted eight years.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Shoku Nihon Kōki'
v.s: In 841, the Japanese court commissioned Fujiwara no Yoshifusa to compile the 'Shoku Nihon Kōki', a history covering the reign of Emperor Ninmyō (833-850). Written in Chinese in the style of Tang court annals, it is one of the Six National Histories of Japan.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong begins persecution of Buddhism
v.s: In 842, Emperor Wuzong, who favored Daoism and faced a treasury shortfall as Buddhist monasteries held tax-exempt land, ordered a nationwide inspection of monastic property. He began by closing smaller temples and forcing monks and nuns to return to lay life, melting down statues for coinage. This persecution reached its peak in 845, known as the 'Huichang Persecution of Buddhism'.

v.t: Uyghur Khaganate completely destroyed; remnants submit to Tang
v.s: In 842, Uyghur Khan Wujie led his remnant troops to raid the Tang frontier but was defeated by Tang generals Liu Mian and Zhang Zhongwu. Wujie was killed, and the Uyghur khans changed frequently until the remnants submitted to the Tang and were settled at Tian De Army (modern Urat Front Banner, Inner Mongolia). The Uyghur Khaganate disappeared from history.

v.t: Kenneth I of Scotland establishes the Kingdom of Alba
v.s: In 842, Kenneth I of the Scots moved his capital from Dunadd to Scone, uniting the Scots and Picts into the Kingdom of Alba, the precursor of the Scottish kingdom. He placed the 'Stone of Destiny' at Scone, where later Scottish kings were crowned.

v.t: Arab physician al-Razi becomes director of Baghdad hospital
v.s: In 842, the famous Arab physician al-Razi was appointed director of the Muqtadiri Hospital in Baghdad. He pioneered clinical observation and wrote the comprehensive 'Kitab al-Hawi' (Continens) and 'On Smallpox and Measles'. He also established separate wards for different diseases, making significant contributions to infectious disease control.

v.t: Treaty of Verdun: Tripartite division of Charlemagne's empire
v.s: In August 843, Charlemagne's three grandsons – Lothair I, Louis the German, and Charles the Bald – signed the Treaty of Verdun, dividing the empire into three parts. Lothair received Middle Francia (a corridor from Italy to the North Sea) and retained the imperial title; Louis received East Francia (east of the Rhine, later Germany); Charles received West Francia (later France). This treaty laid the foundation for the modern borders of Germany, France, and Italy.

v.t: Height of the Huichang persecution: more than 4,000 temples demolished
v.s: In 843, Emperor Wuzong ordered the destruction of all but a few temples nationwide: four each in Chang'an and Luoyang, and one in each capital of a jiedushi. A total of 4,600 temples were demolished, over 260,000 monks and nuns forced to return to lay life, and millions of acres of temple land confiscated; bronze statues were melted for coinage. Buddhism suffered a fatal blow, with only the Chan (Zen) school surviving because of its self-reliance.

v.t: Alfred the Great of Wessex is born
v.s: In 843, Alfred the Great, the future unifier of England, was born into the Wessex royal family. As a child, he accompanied his father on a pilgrimage to Rome, where he was blessed by Pope Leo IV. Alfred would lead the English resistance against the Vikings and lay the foundations for a unified England.

v.t: Great Mosque of Samarra completed
v.s: In 843, Caliph al-Mutawakkil built the Great Mosque of Samarra, then the world's largest mosque. Its spiral minaret (Malwiya) is 52 meters high and a landmark of Iraq. The mosque could accommodate 80,000 worshippers, reflecting the architectural achievements of the Abbasid dynasty.

v.t: Arab fleet captures Bari in Italy
v.s: In 844, the Aghlabid navy captured the southeastern Italian city of Bari, establishing the Emirate of Bari. From this base, they raided the Adriatic coast, threatening the Papal States and Byzantine territories. Bari was recaptured by the Franks in 871, but Islamic influence in southern Europe continued for decades.

v.t: Last Tibetan emperor Lang Darma assassinated; Tibetan empire collapses
v.s: In 844, Lang Darma's persecution of Buddhism led a Buddhist monk named Lhalung Pelgyi Dorje to assassinate him. Tibet then fell into a succession struggle and noble infighting; various tribes became independent, and the Tibetan empire disintegrated, leaving the Tibetan Plateau fragmented for a long period.

v.t: Emperor Wuzong campaigns against Liu Zhen of Ze-Lu
v.s: In 844, Ze-Lu jiedushi Liu Congjian died, and his nephew Liu Zhen took over without permission. Supported by Li Deyu, Wuzong ordered a multi-circuit campaign. After months of siege, a mutiny occurred in Ze-Lu, and Liu Zhen was killed by his own men. The court recovered the five prefectures of Ze-Lu. This victory consolidated central authority over the jiedushi.

v.t: Japan continues compilation of 'Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku'
v.s: In 844, the Japanese court began compiling the 'Nihon Montoku Tennō Jitsuroku', the national history covering the reign of Emperor Montoku (833-850). Written in Chinese, it preserves many historical materials of the early Heian period.

v.t: Huichang persecution of Buddhism reaches its peak
v.s: In July 845, Emperor Wuzong decreed that only four temples be kept nationwide: Xingtang Temple on Chang'an's Left Avenue, Baoshou on Right Avenue, Jing'ai in Luoyang, and Zhuangyan in the supreme capital. All other temples were to be demolished within a set period, with monks and nuns returned to lay life and scriptures burned. The persecution provided the Tang state with land and labor, temporarily increasing fiscal revenue, but it also destroyed cultural heritage; except for the Chan school, all Buddhist sects were severely crippled.

v.t: Vikings first siege Paris
v.s: In March 845, the Danish Viking chieftain Ragnar Lodbrok led 120 ships and over 5,000 men up the Seine to besiege Paris. Charles the Bald, king of West Francia, raised 7,000 pounds of silver as danegeld, and the Vikings withdrew. This was the first large-scale Viking attack on Paris; thereafter Paris was repeatedly threatened.

v.t: Death of Arab philosopher al-Kindi
v.s: In 845, al-Kindi, the first famous philosopher in the Arab world, died in Baghdad. He had translated and commented on numerous Greek philosophical and scientific works and written original treatises, seeking to harmonize reason and revelation, paving the way for the golden age of Islamic philosophy.

v.t: Nanzhao captures Annam Protectorate
v.s: In 845, Nanzhao king Fengyou took advantage of the Tang's internal chaos from the Huichang persecution to capture Jiaozhi again. The Annam protectorate governor Li Hongfu was captured. Nanzhao would control Annam for nearly a decade until the Tang recaptured it.

d.u: Emperor Wuzong of Tang (Li Yan, March) → Emperor Xuānzong of Tang (Li Chen)

v.t: Emperor Wuzong dies; Xuānzong ascends
v.s: In March 846, Emperor Wuzong died of elixir poisoning at age 33. The eunuch Ma Yuanzhi and others enthroned Li Chen, Prince of Guang, as Emperor Xuānzong. Xuānzong had pretended to be insane for 36 years to avoid political persecution. After ascending, he immediately demoted Li Deyu, restored Buddhism, and initiated the 'Dazhong Restoration'.

v.t: Bai Juyi dies
v.s: In August 846, the great Tang realist poet Bai Juyi died in Luoyang at age 75.
