He had served as Minister of Justice and in his later years became a devout Buddhist, known as the 'Fragrant Mountain Recluse'. His poems are known for their simplicity; 'Song of Eternal Sorrow', 'Song of the Pipa', and 'The Old Charcoal Seller' vividly reflect social contradictions. Emperor Xuānzong composed a elegy, praising that 'children recite the Song of Eternal Sorrow, and barbarians sing the Song of the Pipa'.

v.t: Arab army raids the outskirts of Rome
v.s: In 846, Aghlabid troops sallied from Bari to raid the outskirts of Rome, even plundering the treasury of St. Peter's Basilica. Pope Leo IV hastily reinforced the walls, later known as the 'Leonine Wall'. This was the closest an Islamic army ever came to Rome.

v.t: Tibetan nobles split, forming multiple regimes
v.s: In 846, after Lang Darma's death, Tibet had no ruler. Ministers enthroned two young princes, who ruled Lhasa and Shannan. Then local strongmen declared independence, forming regimes such as Ngari, Ladakh, and Guge, plunging the Tibetan Plateau into 400 years of fragmentation.

v.t: Start of the Dazhong Restoration; Xuānzong governs diligently
v.s: In 847, Emperor Xuānzong adopted the era name Dazhong. He took Emperor Taizong as a model, working diligently and reading memorials until late each night; he welcomed remonstrance, even allowing low-level officials to speak directly. He dismissed Li Deyu, appointed Niu faction members, reformed grain transport, and corrected miscarriages of justice. For four years, the Tang showed a revival, known as the 'Dazhong Restoration'.

v.t: Tang restores Buddhist activities
v.s: In 847, Xuānzong announced the repeal of Wuzong's anti-Buddhist decrees, ordering the restoration of monasteries and allowing monks and nuns to reordain. He followed Gaozu's example in honoring Daoism as the state religion but was tolerant of Buddhism. Restored temples were smaller, and the number of clergy was limited to one-third of the pre-persecution level. Buddhism began a slow recovery.

v.t: Caliph al-Mutawakkil succeeds to the Arab throne
v.s: In 847, al-Mutawakkil, son of al-Mu'tasim, became caliph. He abolished the official status of Mu'tazilism, restored Sunni orthodoxy, and released scholars imprisoned by the Inquisition. He undertook grand construction in Samarra, building magnificent palaces and mosques, but his extravagance drained the treasury.

v.t: Charles the Bald of West Francia is crowned
v.s: In 847, Charles the Bald was crowned at Orléans; Pope Leo IV sent legates with a jeweled crown. Charles recognized papal privileges in the Frankish church in exchange for papal support against Viking invasions.

v.t: Xuānzong orders compilation of the 'Sequel to the Essentials of Institutions'
v.s: In 848, Xuānzong ordered Chancellor Cui Xuan to compile the 'Xu Huiyao' in 40 volumes, supplementing the institutional records from Dezong to Wuzong. Based on Su Mian's earlier 'Huiyao', it provides important documentation for the late Tang system.

v.t: Charles the Bald suppresses a rebellion by Aquitaine nobles
v.s: In 848, Duke Pepin II of Aquitaine allied with Vikings and rebelled. Charles the Bald led an army to suppress the revolt, defeating the rebels at Limoges and executing Pepin II. This victory consolidated Charles's rule but also made him more dependent on church and secular nobility.

v.t: Arab astronomer al-Battani begins observations
v.s: In 848, the Arab astronomer al-Battani established an observatory in Raqqa (present-day Syria), beginning forty years of continuous observation. He remeasured the obliquity of the ecliptic, the constant of precession, and the eccentricity of Earth's orbit, producing the 'Sabaean Tables', which directly influenced later European astronomers such as Copernicus.

v.t: Japanese monk Ennin completes 'Record of a Pilgrimage to Tang in Search of the Dharma'
v.s: In 848, Ennin returned to Japan after ten years of pilgrimage in Tang. He wrote the 'Nittō Guhō Junrei Kōki', which records Tang social customs, monastic life, the Huichang persecution, and travel routes, providing invaluable material for the study of Sino-Japanese relations in the Tang era.

v.t: Tang recaptures Qin, Yuan, and Anle prefectures from Tibet
v.s: In 849, due to civil war in Tibet, the generals guarding Qinzhou (present-day Tianshui), Yuanzhou (present-day Guyuan), and Anlezhou submitted to Tang. Emperor Xuānzong sent troops to take over, restoring the three prefectures and moving settlers from Guanzhong to fortify them. This was the first time Tang had regained territory from Tibet since the An Lushan Rebellion.

v.t: Last dated stela at Maya Tikal
v.s: In 849, the last dated stela at Tikal (Maya Long Count 10.3.0.0.0) was erected. After this, Tikal was completely abandoned, marking the definitive end of Classic Maya civilization. The collapse was likely caused by a combination of drought, war, and ecological degradation.

v.t: Arab mathematician al-Battani publishes sine tables
v.s: In 849, al-Battani published tables of sines, replacing Ptolemy's chord tables and greatly simplifying trigonometric calculations. He also discovered the law of sines for spherical triangles, providing convenient tools for astronomical computation.

v.t: Normans establish a permanent settlement, embryonic Duchy of Normandy
v.s: In 849, the Viking chieftain Rollo obtained land in the lower Seine valley and established a permanent residence at Rouen. He allowed his followers to intermarry with Frankish women, adopt the local language, and gradually accept Catholicism. Rollo's state developed into the Duchy of Normandy, one of the most powerful feudal states in 11th-century Europe.

v.t: Zhang Yichao raises the banner of rebellion at Dunhuang, recovers the Hexi Corridor
v.s: In 850 (revolt actually in 848, envoy sent in 850), the local strongman Zhang Yichao, taking advantage of Tibetan civil war, raised the standard of rebellion, expelled the Tibetan governor, and recovered Shazhou (Dunhuang). He sent envoys with maps and household registers, and in 851 they arrived in Chang'an, presenting the map of eleven prefectures of Hexi. Xuānzong appointed Zhang Yichao as jiedushi of the Guiyi Army, ruling the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: Fujiwara no Yoshifusa becomes regent, beginning the Fujiwara regency
v.s: In 850, Emperor Montoku ascended, and his maternal grandfather Fujiwara no Yoshifusa became 'Sesshō' (regent) to the young emperor, holding real power. The Fujiwara clan, using their maternal ties to the imperial family, monopolized the positions of regent and chancellor, ushering in the regency (sekkan) period that lasted nearly two centuries.

v.t: Chola Empire rises in southern India
v.s: In 850, the Chola king Vijayalaya captured Thanjavur and established the Chola Empire. He built a strong navy, controlling the Strait of Malacca and the Bay of Bengal trade, setting the stage for his successors Rajaraja I and Rajendra I to expand the empire to its greatest extent.

v.t: Pagan Kingdom rises in Myanmar
v.s: In 850, the chieftain Pyinbya unified central Myanmar and founded the Pagan Kingdom. He inherited Pyu culture, embraced Theravada Buddhism, and built temples and stupas. Pagan later developed into the most powerful Buddhist empire in Southeast Asia.

v.t: Tang poet Du Mu dies
v.s: In 850 (actually 852), the late Tang poet Du Mu died in Chang'an at age 50. His verse, especially in the seven-character quatrain, is celebrated for its historical themes and evocative imagery; with Li Shangyin he forms the duo 'Little Li Du'.

v.t: Zhang Yichao presents map of eleven Hexi prefectures; Tang establishes Guiyi Army
v.s: In 851, Zhang Yichao sent his older brother Zhang Yitan to Chang'an with maps and household registers of the eleven prefectures, which he presented to the court. Xuānzong was overjoyed, establishing the Guiyi Army in Shazhou with Zhang Yichao as jiedushi, ruling over Dunhuang, Guazhou, Suzhou and other areas. This was the greatest recovery of Hexi by the Tang since the An Lushan Rebellion.

v.t: Arab merchant Sulayman writes the 'Book of China and India'
v.s: In 851, the Arab merchant Sulayman al-Tajir dictated an account that was recorded by others as the 'Book of China and India' (also known as 'Sulayman's Travels'). It describes the sea route from the Persian Gulf to Guangzhou, Tang urban life (such as the foreign quarter in Guangzhou), the emperor's administration, and Buddhist customs, providing an important source for Tang foreign relations.

v.t: Harald Fairhair unifies Norway
v.s: In 851, after the Battle of Hafrsfjord, Harald Fairhair defeated his last rival, essentially unifying the Norwegian chieftains. He then convened an all-island council, enacted national laws, abolished the chieftain system, and established counties. He was thereafter called 'Harald Fairhair', marking the birth of the Norwegian kingdom.

v.t: Indravarman I of Angkor builds Preah Ko temple
v.s: In 851, Indravarman I built Preah Ko at Roluos, dedicated to his ancestors and the Hindu god Shiva. The three statues of the sacred bull Nandi in front of the temple give it its name. Preah Ko is a representative of early Angkorian architecture, with brick structure, stucco reliefs, and high towers, establishing the basis of the Angkor style.

v.t: Xuānzong sends troops to suppress Tangut rebellion
v.s: In 852, the Tanguts rebelled in Xiazhou (present-day Jingbian, Shaanxi), raiding Yanzhou and Yinzhou. Xuānzong sent Wang Zai, defense commander of Yanzhou, to attack; he routed the Tanguts and captured their leader. The court then pacified the Tanguts, reducing their taxes and resettling some inside the border.

v.t: Formal establishment of Norwegian monarchy
v.s: In 852, Harald Fairhair officially crowned himself king of Norway, making Bergen his capital. He established hereditary earldoms, divided administrative regions, and sent expeditions to explore Iceland and the Faroe Islands. The Norwegian monarchy has endured to the present.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Bunka Shūreishū' anthology of Chinese poetry
v.s: In 852, Emperor Montoku commissioned the compilation of the 'Bunka Shūreishū' in three volumes, containing Chinese poems by 80 poets, mainly aristocrats like Fujiwara no Yoshifusa. The refined style reflects the high maturity of Heian aristocratic culture.

v.t: Macedonian dynasty established in the Byzantine Empire
v.s: In 852, Basil I was appointed co-emperor by Byzantine Emperor Michael III. Basil, born a peasant in the Macedonian theme, would found the Macedonian dynasty (867-1056), the second golden age of Byzantium, which would compile the 'Basilika' law code and reconquer Italy and the Balkans.

v.t: Compilation of the 'Dazhong Penal Law Categories'
v.s: In 853, Emperor Xuānzong ordered Zhang Gui and others to compile the 'Dazhong Xingfa Tonglei', which classified Tang statutes, ordinances, formulas, and edicts by subject into 60 volumes, arranged under departments such as Revenue, Justice, and War. This innovation in legal codification facilitated reference and influenced the Song 'Tonglei' compilations.

v.t: Caliph al-Mutawakkil persecutes non-Muslims
v.s: In 853, al-Mutawakkil ordered the destruction of all newly built Christian churches, forced Christians and Jews to wear yellow patches on their clothes, and forbade non-Muslims from holding high office. This policy caused many Copts to convert to Islam, and Christian communities in Egypt rapidly shrank.

v.t: West Francia suppresses a peasant uprising
v.s: In 853, due to Viking raids and heavy taxation, peasants in West Francia rose in revolt. The rebels called themselves 'communes', attacking monasteries and killing lords. Charles the Bald sent troops to suppress the revolt and restore order, though he promised some tax relief to the peasants.

v.t: Death of Nanzhao king Fengyou; Shilong succeeds
v.s: In 853, Nanzhao king Fengyou died and was succeeded by his son Shilong. Shilong proclaimed himself emperor, adopted the state name 'Dali', and openly broke relations with the Tang. In 857, he formally attacked Tang's Xichuan and Annam, beginning a twenty-year war.

v.t: Bulgarian Khan Boris I accepts Orthodox Christianity
v.s: In 854, Bulgarian Khan Boris I signed a peace treaty with Byzantium and accepted Orthodox missionaries. Although he was not immediately baptized, he allowed Christianity to spread in Bulgaria. Boris formally converted in 864, building churches in Pliska, beginning Bulgaria's Christianization.

v.t: Byzantine general Narses pacifies Italian rebels
v.s: In 854, Byzantine territories in Italy were threatened by Lombards and Arabs. The eunuch Narses, under orders from Basil I, led an army into Italy, defeating the Lombards and recapturing Bari, allowing Byzantium to retain control over much of Italy for another century.

v.t: Tang restores the Five Circuits of Lingnan
v.s: In 854, to strengthen control over Lingnan and Annam, Xuānzong restored the Lingnan Five Circuits, overseeing the five jurisdictions of Guang, Gui, Rong, Yong, and Jiao. The position was held by a eunuch responsible for border defense and maritime trade management.

v.t: Arab geographer Ibn Khordadbeh publishes 'Book of Roads and Kingdoms'
v.s: In 854, Ibn Khordadbeh completed the 'Kitab al-Masalik wa al-Mamalik', describing the postal routes, tax districts, sea and land trade routes, and countries along them throughout the Arab Empire. It provides a detailed picture of the trade network from Spain to China and is a crucial source for medieval commercial history.

v.t: East Frankish kingdom takes shape
v.s: In 855, Louis the German convened an imperial council at Frankfurt, demanding that all bishops and counts of East Francia swear allegiance to him alone. He established primogeniture, dividing the territory equally among his three sons. This marked the definitive separation of East Francia from West Francia, becoming the precursor to the German kingdom.

v.t: Pope Nicholas I ascends, asserts papal supremacy
v.s: In 855, the intransigent Nicholas I was elected pope. He asserted that papal authority transcended that of secular rulers, intervened in marriage law, and defended church independence. He came into conflict with Lothair II over a divorce case and even excommunicated his courtiers, establishing the high authority of the medieval papacy.

v.t: Nanzhao's Shilong captures Yongzhou
v.s: In 855, Nanzhao emperor Shilong captured Yongzhou (present-day Nanning), killing over 2,000 Tang defenders and capturing tens of thousands. The Yongzhou protector-general fled. After this, Nanzhao frequently harassed Lingnan, forcing the Tang to station heavy forces there.

v.t: Japan completes 'Shoku Nihon Kōki'
v.s: In 855, the Japanese court completed the 'Shoku Nihon Kōki' in 20 volumes, covering the years 833-850 (reign of Emperor Ninmyō). Edited by Fujiwara no Yoshifusa and others in Chinese annalistic style, it is one of the Six National Histories of Japan.

v.t: Xuānzong sends troops to suppress the Annam rebellion
v.s: In 856, a local chieftain in Annam named Yang Qing colluded with Nanzhao to rebel. Xuānzong ordered Annam protector-general Li Zhuo to attack, and the rebellion was put down the following year. The Tang strengthened its garrison in Annam, repaired fortifications, and stabilized the southwestern frontier.

v.t: Anarchy at Samarra intensifies in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 856, Caliph al-Mutawakkil was assassinated by his Turkic guards. The Abbasid dynasty then fell into a decade of turmoil, with four caliphs killed or deposed in succession, the Turkic guards dominating court politics, and provincial governors becoming independent. The empire existed in name only.

v.t: King Ethelwulf of Wessex makes pilgrimage to Rome
v.s: In 856, King Ethelwulf of Wessex, accompanied by his young son Alfred, traveled to Rome to visit Pope Benedict III. Ethelwulf stayed in Rome for a year, donating gold and silver to the church and receiving the pope's blessing. This strengthened ties between England and the papacy.

v.t: Rurik dynasty establishes Novgorod in Kievan Rus'
v.s: In 856 (according to the 'Primary Chronicle'), the Varangian chieftain Rurik was invited by Slavic tribes to rule and established his rule in Novgorod. Before his death, Rurik entrusted the realm to his relative Oleg, who later captured Kiev and founded Kievan Rus'.

v.t: Charles the Bald of West Francia puts down a rebellion by his son
v.s: In 857, Charles the Bald put down a rebellion led by his son Louis. Louis allied with nobles from Aquitaine and Burgundy, but Charles defeated him and confined him to a monastery. However, the succession crisis in the Carolingian dynasty continued.

v.t: Li Deyu dies in exile on Hainan Island
v.s: In 857, former chancellor Li Deyu died in Yazhou on Hainan Island at age 63. One of the most capable chancellors of the late Tang, he had assisted Emperor Wuzong in reducing the jiedushi and persecuting Buddhism, but was demoted by Xuānzong. Even in exile, he remained concerned about the state. Ten years after his death, his official positions were posthumously restored.

v.t: Fujiwara no Yoshifusa becomes Grand Minister of State
v.s: In 857, Fujiwara no Yoshifusa was appointed Grand Minister of State (Daijō-daijin), the highest administrative post in Japan. He later also received the title 'Sesshō' (regent), ushering in the peak of the Fujiwara regency, with the emperor becoming a figurehead.

v.t: Arab physician al-Razi distinguishes smallpox from measles
v.s: In 857, al-Razi in his book 'On Smallpox and Measles' pointed out that the two diseases are distinct, describing their symptoms, progression, and treatments. This was the first differential diagnosis of infectious diseases in history, and the book was later translated into Latin and widely used in Europe.

v.t: Photian Schism in Byzantium
v.s: In 858, Byzantine Emperor Michael III deposed Patriarch Ignatios and appointed the layman Photios as patriarch. Pope Nicholas I refused to recognize Photios, and Photios declared the pope's intervention illegal. Both sides excommunicated each other, creating the 'Photian Schism'. Although temporarily healed in 867, it exacerbated the East-West tensions that led to the Great Schism of 1054.

v.t: Death of Li Shangyin
v.s: In 858, the late Tang poet Li Shangyin died in his hometown Xingyang, at about age 46. His career was impeded by the Niu-Li factional strife. His poems are allusive and subtle, such as 'The Brocade Zither' and 'Untitled', with elusive imagery and melancholic emotion. Together with Du Mu, they are known as the 'Little Li Du', representing the highest achievement of late Tang poetry.

v.t: Qiu Fu uprising in Xuanzhou, Tang
v.s: In 858 (actually 859), the peasant rebellion led by Qiu Fu broke out in Xuanzhou (present-day Xuancheng, Anhui). Qiu was originally a salt smuggler; he rose up in response to government salt controls, capturing Shanxian, Tangxing, and other counties, amassing tens of thousands of followers, and proclaimed himself 'Army Commander of All under Heaven'. This was the first large-scale peasant rebellion in the late Tang, foreshadowing even greater upheavals.

v.t: Magyars raid Italy
v.s: In 858, Magyar horsemen crossed the Alps and plundered Pavia, Verona, and other cities in northern Italy, burning churches and capturing inhabitants for ransom. For decades thereafter, the Magyars annually raided Central Europe and Italy, becoming a scourge of Christendom.

d.u: Emperor Xuānzong of Tang (Li Chen, before August) → Emperor Yizong of Tang (Li Cui)

v.t: Emperor Xuānzong dies; Yizong ascends
v.s: In August 859, Emperor Xuānzong died at age 50 in the Hanyuan Hall. He had ruled diligently for 13 years, a period known as the 'Dazhong Restoration'. However, his son Emperor Yizong immediately indulged in pleasure and allowed eunuchs to dominate, plunging the Tang into rapid decline.

v.t: Qiu Fu rebellion expands, capturing prefectures in eastern Zhejiang
v.s: In December 859, Qiu Fu captured Shanxian (present-day Shengzhou, Zhejiang), and his rebel army grew to 30,000. They looted government granaries and distributed grain to the poor, attracting many to join. Qiu proclaimed the state of 'Luoping' with the era name 'Dashun', shaking eastern Zhejiang. The Tang sent Wang Shi, the surveillance commissioner of eastern Zhejiang, to suppress the rebellion, which was defeated the following year.

v.t: Oleg of Kievan Rus' conquers Smolensk
v.s: In 859, Oleg, successor to Rurik in Novgorod, led an army down the Dnieper and captured Smolensk and Lyubech, gaining control of the 'Varangian to Greek' trade route from the Baltic to the Black Sea. Oleg is considered the true founder of Kievan Rus'.

v.t: Indravarman I of Angkor builds Bakong temple
v.s: In 859, Indravarman I built Bakong at Roluos as the state temple of the Angkor dynasty. Bakong is a five-tiered pyramid-shaped temple symbolizing Mount Meru, the center of the universe, dedicated to Shiva. It was the first large sandstone structure in the Angkor region, inaugurating the tradition of magnificent Angkorian temple architecture.

v.t: Nanzhao captures Jiaozhi; Tang loses Annam
v.s: In 860, Nanzhao emperor Shilong captured Jiaozhi (present-day Hanoi), and the Annam protector-general fled. Nanzhao occupied Annam for twelve years, levying taxes and conscripting troops, and harassed Lingnan. The Tang sent forces to recover the region, but the back-and-forth war consumed enormous resources.

v.t: Norse discovery and settlement of Iceland
v.s: In 860, the Norwegian Viking Ingólfr Arnarson, after being exiled for killing a man, sailed to Iceland with his family. He established a permanent settlement at Reykjavík (meaning 'Smoky Bay') in the southwest, becoming the first permanent Icelandic colonist. Subsequently, more Norwegians arrived, and the Icelandic parliament (Althing) was founded in 930.

v.t: First Rus' attack on Constantinople
v.s: In June 860, the Rus' chieftains Askold and Dir led a fleet of over 200 ships to besiege Constantinople. The Byzantine defenders panicked and prayed for the Virgin's intervention. The Rus' plundered the suburbs and then withdrew; the Byzantines credited a miraculous salvation. Afterward, Byzantium and the Rus' signed a commercial treaty granting the Rus' duty-free trade rights.

v.t: Tang and Tibet continue skirmishes in Hexi
v.s: In 860, Zhang Yichao of the Guiyi Army pushed eastward against the Tibetan-held Liangzhou (present-day Wuwei), which he captured after a three-year siege in 863. The Tang recovered control of the Hexi Corridor, and the remaining Tibetan tribes dispersed and retreated into Qinghai.

v.t: Byzantine Emperor Michael III assassinated; Basil I ascends
v.s: In 861, co-emperor Basil I plotted the assassination of Michael III and proclaimed himself sole emperor, founding the Macedonian dynasty (867-1056). Basil, of peasant origin who rose through court intrigue, reformed the military and navy, compiled the 'Basilika' code, and suppressed Arab pirates, laying the foundation for Byzantium's second golden age.

v.t: Nanzhao besieges Chengdu; Tang holds out
v.s: In 861, Nanzhao emperor Shilong laid siege to Chengdu. Yan Qingfu, jiedushi of Xichuan, relied on the strong city walls to defend. After several months, the Nanzhao army ran out of supplies and withdrew. The Tang sent reinforcements to Xichuan, but Nanzhao's repeated harassment devastated the local economy.

v.t: Abbasid caliph al-Musta'in deposed
v.s: In 861, the Turkic guards in Samarra deposed Caliph al-Musta'in and installed al-Mu'tazz. The Turkic guards thereafter arbitrarily deposed and installed caliphs, and the Abbasid central authority crumbled. Provincial governors such as the Tahirids, Saffarids, and Tulunids became independent, and the empire existed in name only.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'New National History'
v.s: In 861, the Japanese court began compiling the 'Shin Kokushi' (also known as 'Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'), a history of the reigns of Emperors Seiwa, Yōzei, and Kōkō. Edited by Fujiwara no Tokihira and others, it was the last of the Six National Histories, completed in 901.

v.t: Rurik and his brothers establish the Principality of Novgorod
v.s: In 862, according to the 'Primary Chronicle', Slavic and Finnish tribes, beset by internal strife, invited the Varangian chieftain Rurik and his brothers to rule. Rurik established his seat in Novgorod; his brother Sineus settled at Beloozero and Truvor at Izborsk. The Rurikid dynasty is traditionally considered the beginning of the Russian state.

v.t: Arab physician al-Razi becomes director of Baghdad hospital
v.s: In 862, al-Razi was appointed director of the Muqtadiri Hospital in Baghdad. He was the first to divide a hospital into wards for internal medicine, surgery, orthopedics, and ophthalmology, establish isolation rooms, and build a medical library. He also wrote the 'Kitab al-Hawi', the most comprehensive medical encyclopedia of his time.

v.t: First appearance of Japanese pirates along the Tang coast
v.s: In 862, Japanese pirates (wakō) harassed the southeast coast of Tang, raiding Mingzhou (present-day Ningbo) and Taizhou. Tang local officials strengthened coastal defense by building beacon towers, but the wakō problem intensified in the late Tang.

v.t: King Ethelbert of Wessex ascends
v.s: In 862, King Ethelbert of Wessex (brother of Alfred) inherited the throne. He continued the resistance against Viking incursions, but in 865 a large Viking army invaded England, and Ethelbert fought alongside his brother Alfred.

v.t: Nanzhao again captures Jiaozhi; Tang loses Annam
v.s: In 863, Nanzhao forces, cooperating with local natives, recaptured Jiaozhi; the Tang protector Cai Xi was killed, and his family taken prisoner. Nanzhao occupied all of Annam, establishing administrative structures and collecting taxes. The Tang mobilized troops from Lingnan to counterattack, but repeatedly failed.

v.t: Cyril and Methodius go to Moravia to preach
v.s: In 863, Byzantine Emperor Michael III sent the brothers Cyril and Methodius to Great Moravia to spread Christianity. Cyril created the Glagolitic alphabet (later evolving into Cyrillic) and translated the Bible into Slavic. Their mission laid the foundation for Slavic Christian culture.

v.t: Pang Xun rebellion breaks out
v.s: In 863 (actually 868), the Tang government mobilized soldiers from Xuzhou to garrison Guizhou (present-day Guilin) to defend against Nanzhao. They were supposed to be rotated after three years, but after six years they were still not allowed to return. Led by Pang Xun, the garrison troops mutinied and made their way back north, swelling to tens of thousands along the route, wreaking havoc in the Huai and Yangtze regions and severely damaging Tang rule.

v.t: Construction of the famous spiral minaret in Samarra
v.s: In 863, the spiral Malwiya minaret of the Great Mosque of Samarra was completed. It is 52 meters high, with a square base and a spiraling ramp. This architectural form may have been inspired by the Babylonian ziggurat and remains the most famous architectural monument of the Abbasid dynasty.

v.t: Bulgarian Khan Boris I officially accepts Orthodox Christianity
v.s: In 864, under Byzantine military pressure, Bulgarian Khan Boris I chose to accept Orthodox Christianity and was publicly baptized in Pliska. Byzantium sent missionaries to establish a church organization in Bulgaria, but Boris also sought papal support to maintain a degree of autocephaly, gaining the Bulgarian church autonomous status.

v.t: Gao Pian leads army to recapture Annam
v.s: In 864, Emperor Yizong appointed the skilled general Gao Pian as Annam protector. Gao defeated the Nanzhao fleet at sea, landed at Haimen (present-day Hai Phong, Vietnam), and after a year of hard fighting recaptured Jiaozhi, killing the Nanzhao commander. Gao built Dalou City (present-day Hanoi) as the center of Tang rule in northern Vietnam.

v.t: Cyril and Methodius obtain papal approval in Rome
v.s: In 864, the brothers Cyril and Methodius traveled to Rome with relics of St. Clement to seek papal approval for Slavic liturgy. Pope Adrian II granted their request, consecrating the Slavic Bible. Cyril died in Rome, and his brother Methodius continued missionary work.

v.t: Indravarman I of Angkor dies; Yasovarman I succeeds
v.s: In 864, King Indravarman I of Angkor died and was succeeded by his son Yasovarman I. Yasovarman moved the capital from Roluos to the Angkor region, beginning construction of the famous Phnom Bakheng temple and the Angkor water management system.

v.t: Great Viking Army invades England
v.s: In 865, the Viking chieftains Ivar the Boneless and Halfdan Ragnarsson landed the 'Great Heathen Army' on the east coast of England. Unlike previous raids, this army intended to conquer and settle. They quickly captured York and established the Viking kingdom of 'Jorvik'.

v.t: Pang Xun rebellion in Tang
v.s: In 865 (actually 868), Pang Xun, leading the Guizhou garrison troops on their northward march, captured Suzhou and Xuzhou, and his forces grew to tens of thousands. He proclaimed himself 'Heavenly General', seized the Huainan region, and threatened the eastern capital Luoyang. The Tang mobilized several circuits to suppress him. Pang was killed in battle the following year.

v.t: Turkic slave soldiers seize power in Samarra
v.s: In 865, the Turkic guards in Samarra again deposed a caliph and installed al-Muhtadi. The Abbasid central government completely lost control over the provinces; Egypt's Tulunid dynasty, Persia's Saffarids, Central Asia's Samanids, and others became de facto independent. The Arab Empire entered a period of fragmentation.

v.t: Emperor Seiwa of Japan ascends; Fujiwara no Mototsune becomes regent
v.s: In 865, nine-year-old Emperor Seiwa ascended, and his maternal grandfather Fujiwara no Mototsune became regent. Mototsune forced the emperor to entrust all state affairs to him, requiring all edicts to bear his seal. The regency (sekkan) system became further entrenched.

v.t: Macedonian dynasty officially established in Byzantium
v.s: In 866, Basil I was crowned sole Byzantine emperor, founding the Macedonian dynasty (867-1056). He reformed the army, establishing a central field army; compiled the 'Procheiron' law code protecting small farmers; and sent the navy to recover Crete and Cyprus. The Macedonian dynasty was Byzantium's second golden age.

v.t: Gao Pian captures Jiaozhi; Nanzhao expelled from Annam
v.s: In 866, Gao Pian broke the last Nanzhao stronghold in Annam, killing the Nanzhao commander Duan Qianqiu. The Tang restored the Annam Protectorate, and Gao Pian built Dalou City as a fortified military base. Nanzhao never again invaded Annam, instead turning to harass Xichuan.

v.t: Northumbria falls to the Vikings
v.s: In 866, the Viking army captured the Northumbrian capital York, and King Ella was killed. Northumbria became a Viking colony, and Ivar the Boneless ruled in York as king. Northeastern England fell under Viking control.

v.t: Birth of Arab philosopher al-Farabi
v.s: In 866, al-Farabi was born in Farab, Central Asia (present-day Kazakhstan). Later known as the 'Second Teacher' (after Aristotle), he wrote works such as 'On the Intellect' and 'The Virtuous City', advocating knowledge of God through reason, profoundly influencing Avicenna and European scholasticism.

v.t: Peak of the Arab Translation Movement
v.s: In 867, the translation movement at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad reached its zenith. Hunayn ibn Ishaq translated Aristotle's 'Organon' and Galen's medical works, and wrote original scientific treatises. The House of Wisdom housed hundreds of thousands of books, making it the world's knowledge center.

v.t: King Ethelred I of Wessex fights the Vikings
v.s: In 867, King Ethelred I of Wessex and his brother Alfred defeated the Viking army at the Battle of Ashdown. Despite the victory, Wessex suffered heavy losses and could not prevent the Vikings from occupying Northumbria and Mercia.

v.t: Tibetan nobles establish the Guge kingdom in Ngari
v.s: In 867, a descendant of the Tibetan royal line, Kyide Nyimagon, fled to the Ngari region and was acclaimed king, establishing the Guge kingdom. Guge revered Buddhism, sending monks to study in India, becoming a source of the 'later dissemination' of Tibetan Buddhism.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Sequel to the Chronicles of Japan'
v.s: In 867, the Japanese court began compiling the 'Shoku Nihongi', covering the years 697-791 to supplement the 'Nihon Shoki'. The work is in Chinese annalistic style, 40 volumes, the largest of the Six National Histories, taking 12 years to complete.

v.t: The Diamond Sutra printed – the earliest complete printed book in the world
v.s: On 15 April 868 (Xiantong 9th Year), Wang Jie financed the printing of the 'Diamond Sutra' to pray for his parents. The scroll is 5.25 meters long, made of seven sheets of paper pasted together, with exquisite carving and clear printing, including a frontispiece depicting the Buddha. It is the world's earliest complete printed book with a precise date. Discovered in the Mogao Caves in 1900, it now resides in the British Library.

v.t: Pang Xun rebellion defeated
v.s: In the autumn of 868, Tang armies converged on Xuzhou, and Pang Xun was defeated and killed. Although the rebellion failed, it severely weakened the Tang, and among its leaders were later Huang Chao's generals Shang Rang and Wang Xianzhi. After the rebellion, the Tang could no longer control the Huai grain transport, and the treasury was on the brink of collapse.

v.t: Alfred the Great ascends to the throne of Wessex
v.s: In 868, King Ethelred I of Wessex died of wounds sustained in battle against the Vikings, and his brother Alfred succeeded, known as Alfred the Great. He was only 19 years old and faced the imminent threat of a full Viking invasion.

v.t: Abbasid capital moved back from Samarra to Baghdad
v.s: In 868, Caliph al-Mu'tamid moved the capital back from Samarra to Baghdad, ending 56 years of the 'Anarchy at Samarra'. But the Abbasid central government was too weak to recover, and the governors of Egypt, Persia, and Central Asia were completely independent, the empire existing only in name.

v.t: Charles the Bald of West Francia strengthens royal power
v.s: In 869, taking advantage of the death of East Frankish king Louis the German, Charles the Bald invaded Lotharingia, claiming inheritance rights. He forced Lotharingian nobles to swear allegiance and was crowned king of Lotharingia. This led to conflict with the new East Frankish king, and the two sides eventually partitioned Lotharingia by treaty.

v.t: Tang's southwestern border crisis intensifies as Nanzhao invades again
v.s: In 869, Nanzhao emperor Shilong besieged Chengdu; Xichuan jiedushi Lu Dan mounted a desperate defense, but the city ran out of food and the people ate roots. The Tang dispatched reinforcements from Dongchuan and Shannan West, and Nanzhao withdrew. Afterward, Nanzhao raided annually, laying waste to the southwestern region.

v.t: Methodius appointed bishop of Pannonia
v.s: In 869, Pope Adrian II appointed Methodius as bishop of Pannonia, allowing Slavic liturgy. However, the Bavarian episcopate opposed Slavic use and imprisoned Methodius for two years. Pope John VIII later freed him and confirmed his position.

v.t: Zanj Rebellion breaks out in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 869, a slave rebellion (the Zanj) broke out in southern Iraq, led by Ali ibn Muhammad, who called for the destruction of the Abbasid regime. The rebels captured Basra and other cities, controlling the marshlands of southern Mesopotamia for fourteen years, dealing a severe blow to the Abbasid dynasty.

v.t: Treaty of Meersen partitions Lotharingia
v.s: In 870, Charles the Bald of West Francia and the sons of Louis the German of East Francia signed the Treaty of Meersen, dividing the territory of the late Lothair II of Middle Francia. Charles received western Lotharingia (present-day French Lorraine), and Louis received the eastern part (present-day German Rhineland). With this, Charlemagne's empire was partitioned into the three modern states.

v.t: Observatory built in Baghdad by the Arab Empire
v.s: In 870, Caliph al-Mu'tamid built a large observatory in Baghdad, equipped with quadrants, astrolabes, and other instruments, directed by astronomer Muhammad al-Battani. Al-Battani conducted observations there for 41 years, obtaining accurate solar orbital parameters, which were cited by Copernicus.

v.t: Alfred the Great makes peace with the Vikings
v.s: In 870, Alfred the Great paid the Vikings to stop their attacks on Wessex. He used the precious peace to fortify strongholds, train cavalry, and build a fleet in preparation for a counteroffensive.

v.t: Nanzhao breaks through the outer wall of Chengdu and abducts artisans
v.s: In 870, Nanzhao forces broke through the outer wall of Chengdu, plundering for ten days and capturing tens of thousands of silk weavers, artisans, and monks, transporting them back to Yunnan. This advanced Nanzhao's textile industry while devastating the Tang's Xichuan economy.

v.t: Alfred the Great defeats the Vikings at the Battle of Ashdown
v.s: In 871, Alfred of Wessex defeated the Viking army at Ashdown, killing five Danish earls. But due to a shortage of troops, Alfred was forced to sue for peace and pay danegeld. The same year he was formally crowned king of Wessex, and he reorganized the army, built burhs, and prepared for the restoration of England.

v.t: Emperor Yizong welcomes the Buddha relic, spending extravagantly
v.s: In 871, Emperor Yizong sent envoys to bring the Buddha relic from Famen Temple to Chang'an for grand ceremonies, using gold and silver reliquaries, dragon brain incense, and other luxuries. Officials competed to donate, and the populace impoverished themselves to offer. Yizong ignored the empty treasury and lavishly rewarded monks and eunuchs, worsening government corruption.

v.t: Byzantine Basil I defeats Arab pirates
v.s: In 871, Emperor Basil I of Byzantium sent the navy to destroy a large Arab pirate fleet in the Aegean, ending the threat to the Greek coast. He also recovered Crete (actually recaptured in 961) and regained control of the eastern Mediterranean.

v.t: Emperor Yōzei of Japan ascends; Fujiwara no Mototsune continues as regent
v.s: In 871, nine-year-old Emperor Yōzei ascended, and his maternal grandfather Fujiwara no Mototsune remained as regent. Mototsune placed his daughter in the imperial palace as empress, further controlling the court. When Yōzei came of age and tried to rule personally, Mototsune forced him to abdicate and installed his great-uncle, Emperor Kōkō.

v.t: Zanj rebels capture Basra
v.s: In 872, the Zanj rebel leader Ali ibn Muhammad captured Basra, massacred the population, and burned merchant ships. The rebels controlled southern Mesopotamia, cutting Baghdad off from the Persian Gulf. The Abbasids sent several punitive expeditions, all failed, and were forced to negotiate.

v.t: Alfred the Great rebuilds London
v.s: In 872, Alfred the Great recaptured London from the Vikings and ordered the rebuilding of its walls and port. He declared London a free port, encouraging domestic and foreign merchants to trade there. London gradually became England's economic center.

v.t: On the eve of the Wang Xianzhi and Huang Chao rebellion
v.s: In 872, a drought struck the eastern regions; the people subsisted on wild grass while the government still demanded taxes. Wang Xianzhi, a salt smuggler from Puzhou (present-day Juancheng, Shandong), began secretly organizing followers, and Huang Chao also participated. The great peasant rebellion of the late Tang was about to erupt.

v.t: Harald Fairhair of Norway claims Iceland
v.s: In 872, after unifying Norway, King Harald Fairhair declared Iceland a Norwegian possession, but without stationing troops, Iceland remained effectively autonomous.

d.u: Emperor Yizong of Tang (Li Cui, August) → Emperor Xizong of Tang (Li Xuan)

v.t: Emperor Yizong dies; Xizong ascends
v.s: In August 873, Emperor Yizong died. His fifth son, Li Xuan, was enthroned as Emperor Xizong, only 12 years old. The eunuch Tian Lingzi took control of the government, calling himself 'adoptive father', and Xizong became a puppet. Tang's decline became irreversible.

v.t: Death of Harald Fairhair of Norway
v.s: In 873, Harald Fairhair, the first king of a unified Norway, died, and his son Eric I succeeded. Eric was brutal and warlike, called 'Bloodaxe' by the nobles. Norway then fell into civil war until his grandson Haakon I restored unity.

v.t: Saffarid dynasty gains independence in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 873, the eastern Persian warlord Ya'qub ibn al-Layth al-Saffar captured Herat and Kerman, establishing the Saffarid dynasty independent of the Abbasids. He marched west, even reaching near Baghdad, forcing the caliph to recognize his rule.

v.t: Yasovarman I of Angkor moves the capital to Angkor
v.s: In 873, Yasovarman I decided to move the capital from Roluos to the Angkor region, building a national temple on Phnom Bakheng. He also excavated the East Baray reservoir to ensure water supply. Angkor became the capital of the Khmer Empire for nearly 500 years.

v.t: Wang Xianzhi and Huang Chao rebellion erupts
v.s: In 874, the salt smuggler Wang Xianzhi raised a rebel army at Changyuan (present-day Changyuan, Henan), proclaiming himself 'Heavenly Equalizing General', issuing a manifesto denouncing Tang misrule. Within months, tens of thousands responded, capturing Cao and Pu prefectures. The same year, Huang Chao gathered followers in Yuanju (present-day Heze, Shandong) to join him. The great peasant uprising of the late Tang had begun.

v.t: Samanid dynasty rises in Transoxiana
v.s: In 874, Nasr I, of the Samanid family, was recognized as governor of Transoxiana by the caliph, with his capital at Bukhara. The Samanids patronized Islam, employed Persian scholars and writers, fostering a Persian-Islamic cultural renaissance that laid the foundation for later Persian literature.

v.t: Peak of Viking settlement in Iceland
v.s: In 874, Iceland experienced a wave of immigration. Norwegian nobles opposed to Harald Fairhair emigrated en masse, with over 400 ships arriving that year, establishing settlements at Reykjavík, Akranes, and elsewhere. The Icelandic Althing was founded in 930.

v.t: Byzantine Empire promulgates the 'Basilika'
v.s: In 874, Basil I promulgated the 'Basilika', a simplification and Greek translation of the Justinian Code. In 60 volumes, it was adapted to the social changes of 9th-century Byzantium and remained in use until 1453.

v.t: Wang Xianzhi campaigns through Central China, capturing Yizhou
v.s: In 875, Wang Xianzhi captured Yizhou (present-day Linyi, Shandong), killing the prefect and distributing grain from government granaries. The Tang court sent Pinglu jiedushi Song Wei to suppress him, but both sides suffered losses. Wang, known for his bravery, was acclaimed 'General Wang Xianzhi' by his followers.

v.t: Alfred the Great wins a great victory over the Vikings at Edington
v.s: In 875, Alfred the Great defeated the Viking army at Edington (present-day Wiltshire), forcing the Viking leader Guthrum to accept the Treaty of Wedmore, which divided England into two parts: the south under Wessex and the north (the 'Danelaw') under the Vikings. Alfred's victory prevented the complete Viking conquest of England.

v.t: Zanj rebels occupy Wasit
v.s: In 875, the Zanj rebels captured the strategic city of Wasit in Mesopotamia and killed its governor. The Abbasid caliph was forced to recognize the rebels' control over the southern region and paid them tribute. Although the Zanj did not overthrow the dynasty, they brought it to financial exhaustion.

v.t: Yasovarman I of Angkor builds the East Baray reservoir
v.s: In 875, Yasovarman I began constructing the East Baray reservoir, 7 km long and 1.8 km wide, with a capacity of 55 million cubic meters. A canal fed it from the Siem Reap River, ensuring agricultural irrigation and urban water supply, allowing Angkor to support a population of one million.

v.t: Huang Chao and Wang Xianzhi join forces, strengthening the rebel army
v.s: In 876, Huang Chao with tens of thousands of troops joined Wang Xianzhi at Qizhou (present-day Qichun, Hubei), merging into a force of over 100,000. They campaigned through Hubei, Henan, and Anhui, capturing many Tang counties. The rebels used mobile warfare, exhausting the government forces.

v.t: Louis II of West Francia dies; Carolingian decline
v.s: In 876, Louis II of West Francia (son of Charles the Bald) died without heirs. The nobles elected Odo, Count of Paris, as king, ending Carolingian rule in West Francia. This marked the decline of the Carolingian dynasty and the start of the Capetian era in France.

v.t: Saffarids occupy Fars in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 876, Saffarid ruler Ya'qub conquered Fars province (southern Iran), controlling the Persian Gulf coast. He minted his own coins and omitted the caliph's name from the Friday sermon, becoming effectively independent.

v.t: Gao Pian defeated by Huang Chao, retreats to Yangzhou
v.s: In 876, Emperor Xizong appointed Gao Pian as supreme commander of all circuit armies to suppress Huang Chao. Gao was defeated by Huang at Sizhou (present-day Sixian, Anhui), suffering heavy losses, and withdrew to Yangzhou, after which he remained passive and preserved his forces.

v.t: Wang Xianzhi is killed in battle; Huang Chao unites the rebels
v.s: In the first month of 877, Wang Xianzhi was defeated and killed by the Tang general Zeng Yuanyu at Huangmei (present-day Hubei). His remaining troops, led by Shang Rang and Chai Cun, joined Huang Chao, who was proclaimed 'Heaven-Punishing General' with the era name 'Wangba'. Huang now led the united rebel forces and became the Tang's most formidable foe.

v.t: Alfred the Great establishes the English navy
v.s: In 877, Alfred the Great ordered the construction of a new type of longship, forming England's first navy. He designed ships that were faster and more stable than Viking longships, each carrying 60 oarsmen, used for coastal patrol and intercepting Viking fleets.

v.t: Zanj rebels control the Mesopotamian marshes
v.s: In 877, the Zanj rebels completely controlled the marshlands of southern Iraq, building strongholds and minting coins. The Abbasid caliph's multiple expeditions failed, and he was forced to recognize the rebels' rule over Basra, Wasit, and other cities.

v.t: Emperor Uda of Japan ascends; Fujiwara clan continues regency
v.s: In 877, Emperor Uda ascended. As he was an adult, Fujiwara no Mototsune could no longer serve as regent but instead became 'Kampaku' (chancellor for an adult emperor). The Kampaku position was thereafter monopolized by the Fujiwara, formalizing the regency system.

v.t: Huang Chao crosses the Yangtze and captures Guangzhou
v.s: In 878, Huang Chao led a half-million-strong army across the Yangtze at Caishiji, sweeping through Jiangnan, capturing Hangzhou, Fuzhou, and other cities. He then turned south, passed through Fujian into Lingnan, and captured Guangzhou at year's end, killing the Lingnan jiedushi Li Tiao. Huang stayed in Guangzhou for several months, recruiting soldiers and gathering supplies.

v.t: Guthrum invades Wessex; Alfred retreats to the marshes
v.s: In January 878, the Viking chieftain Guthrum launched a surprise attack on Wessex, capturing Chippenham. Alfred fled with a small retinue into the marshes of Somerset. There he built a stronghold, rallied the militia, and counterattacked in May, winning a great victory over the Vikings at Edington.

v.t: Tulunid dynasty becomes independent in Egypt
v.s: In 878, the Egyptian governor Ahmad ibn Tulun declared independence, founding the Tulunid dynasty. He built a new capital, al-Qata'i (in the suburbs of present-day Cairo), expanded the Ibn Tulun Mosque, and strengthened the navy, making Egypt a prosperous and independent economic and cultural center.

v.t: Tang eunuch Tian Lingzi monopolizes power, sells offices
v.s: In 878, the eunuch Tian Lingzi, favored by Xizong, became commander of the Shence Army and openly sold offices. Prefects and county magistrates were sold for fixed prices to entertain Xizong. The government became corrupt, and the populace alienated.

v.t: Huang Chao turns north, captures Xiangyang
v.s: In 879, due to tropical disease in his army, Huang Chao decided to abandon Guangzhou and march north. He issued a manifesto accusing the court of corruption, declaring his intention to 'march straight to Chang'an'. The rebels passed through Hunan and Hubei, capturing Xiangyang and threatening the Central Plains.

v.t: Peak of Córdoba under the Umayyads
v.s: In 879, the Umayyad emir Muhammad I expanded the Great Mosque of Córdoba, adding a minaret and prayer hall. Córdoba became the most populous city in Europe (about 250,000), with 70 libraries and hundreds of bathhouses, a beacon of civilization in the Western world.

v.t: Zanj rebels besiege Baghdad
v.s: In 879, the Zanj rebels advanced from the south, threatening the Abbasid capital Baghdad. Caliph al-Mu'tamid appealed for help from the provinces. The Persian Samanids sent an army to break the siege, but the Zanj rebellion was not completely suppressed.

v.t: Tang confers title of Prince of Yunnan on Nanzhao, temporary truce
v.s: In 879, preoccupied by the Huang Chao rebellion, the Tang court negotiated a truce with Nanzhao, awarding Shilong the title 'Prince of Yunnan' and an annual silk tribute. Nanzhao ceased its raids on Xichuan, allowing Tang to concentrate forces against Huang Chao.

v.t: Huang Chao enters Chang'an; Xizong flees west
v.s: In November 880, Huang Chao led a 600,000-strong army through Tong Pass and approached Chang'an. Emperor Xizong, escorted by the eunuch Tian Lingzi, fled with a small entourage to Chengdu. In December, Huang Chao entered Chang'an in a golden carriage and proclaimed himself emperor of the 'Great Qi' dynasty, era name 'Jintong'. This was the second time a Tang emperor fled the capital (the first being during the An Lushan Rebellion).

v.t: Alfred the Great issues the 'Alfredian Code'
v.s: In 880, Alfred the Great issued the 'Alfredian Code', blending the Ten Commandments, canon law, and Anglo-Saxon customary law. It mandated equality before the law (though with class distinctions) and replaced blood feuds with monetary compensation. This was England's first national law code.

v.t: Basil I dies; Leo VI succeeds in Byzantium
v.s: In 880 (actual 886; adjusted here for continuity), Basil I died, and his son Leo VI succeeded. Leo completed the 'Basilika' legal code and commissioned translations of Arabic scientific works. The year 880 saw Basil I defeat the Paulician rebellion, stabilizing the eastern front.

v.t: Sugawara no Michizane appointed professor of literature in Japan
v.s: In 880, the Japanese scholar Sugawara no Michizane was appointed professor of literature, the highest academic post at court. Known for his Chinese poetry, he wrote the 'Kanke Bunso', promoting Japanese sinology. He was later exiled due to political intrigue and posthumously deified as the god of learning.

v.t: Tyranny of Huang Chao's Great Qi regime; chaos in Chang'an
v.s: In 881, Huang Chao implemented radical policies in Chang'an, confiscating the property of the wealthy and slaughtering Tang imperial relatives and officials. The rebels' discipline collapsed, and they plundered everywhere; many residents fled. Tang generals Zheng Tian and Wang Duo organized counterattacks, besieging Chang'an.

v.t: Li Keyong leads Shatuo troops to assist Tang
v.s: In 881, Emperor Xizong appointed Li Keyong, chieftain of the Shatuo Turks, as jiedushi of Yanmen, and he led 10,000 Shatuo cavalry south. Li defeated Huang Chao's forces at Liangtianbei and approached Chang'an, forcing Huang to retreat east. For his services, Li was given the title Prince of Jin, becoming one of the most powerful jiedushi of the late Tang.

v.t: Oleg of Kievan Rus' captures Kiev
v.s: In 881, Oleg led an army south from Novgorod, captured Kiev, and killed the local princes Askold and Dir. Oleg moved the capital to Kiev and took the title 'Grand Prince of Kiev', formally establishing Kievan Rus'. He controlled the Dnieper trade route and opened negotiations with Byzantium.

v.t: Arab philosopher al-Farabi writes 'On the Intellect'
v.s: In 881, al-Farabi completed 'On the Intellect', exploring the relationship between human intellect and God, proposing the theory of the 'active intellect'. He argued that through reason one can attain a transcendent knowledge of God, influencing Avicenna and Thomas Aquinas.

v.t: Zhu Wen defects from Huang Chao to Tang, granted name Quanzhong
v.s: In 882, Zhu Wen, a general under Huang Chao, betrayed him and surrendered to Tang at Tongzhou (present-day Dali, Shaanxi). Emperor Xizong was delighted, granted him the name 'Quanzhong' (Loyalty), and appointed him jiedushi of Xuanwu. Zhu later became the main force in suppressing Huang Chao and would eventually usurp the Tang to establish the Later Liang dynasty.

v.t: Kievan Rus' moves capital to Kiev
v.s: In 882, Oleg officially made Kiev his capital, building walls, palaces, and administrative institutions. Kievan Rus' controlled the trade route from the Baltic to the Black Sea, becoming the most powerful state in Eastern Europe.

v.t: Alfred the Great commissions the 'Anglo-Saxon Chronicle'
v.s: In 882, Alfred the Great ordered the compilation of the 'Anglo-Saxon Chronicle', which recorded the history of England from the Roman invasion to his own time. Written in Old English with annual entries, it is the most valuable source for early English history.

v.t: Japan compiles the 'Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku'
v.s: In 882, the Japanese court began compiling the 'Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku', covering the reigns of Emperors Seiwa, Yōzei, and Kōkō. Edited by Fujiwara no Tokihira and others, it was the last of the Six National Histories, completed in 901.

v.t: Li Keyong recovers Chang'an; Huang Chao flees east
v.s: In April 883, the combined forces of Li Keyong's Shatuo army and Zhu Wen recaptured Chang'an. Huang Chao's forces burned the city and retreated east. Emperor Xizong returned to Chang'an, but the palaces were destroyed, and he had to stay in the mansion of the eunuch Tian Lingzi. Li Keyong was given the title Prince of Jin, and Zhu Wen became Prince of Liang, the two most powerful jiedushi of the late Tang.

v.t: Complete collapse of the Tibetan empire; plateau fragmented
v.s: In 883, after the assassination of Lang Darma and the extinction of the royal line, central Tibet (U-Tsang) split into multiple tribes. Local regimes such as Ngari, Yatse, and Yalong were independent, plunging the Tibetan Plateau into four centuries of division.

v.t: King Odo of West Francia strengthens royal power
v.s: In 883, King Odo of West Francia suppressed a rebellion by nobles, recapturing Paris and Orléans. With the support of the church, he reduced the power of feudal lords, laying the groundwork for early French centralization.

v.t: Simeon I ascends to the Bulgarian throne
v.s: In 883, Khan Boris I of Bulgaria abdicated, and his son Simeon I succeeded. Simeon, educated in Constantinople, would turn Bulgaria into a major Balkan power, repeatedly defeating the Byzantine Empire.

v.t: Huang Chao is defeated and commits suicide; rebellion ends
v.s: In June 884, Huang Chao was surrounded by the combined armies of Li Keyong and Zhu Wen in the Langhu Valley of Mount Tai. His nephew Lin Yan beheaded Huang and surrendered, but Lin was soon killed as well. Although the rebellion failed, it severely weakened the Tang, allowing the jiedushi to carve out territories, and the Tang existed only in name.

v.t: Alfred the Great issues an education decree
v.s: In 884, Alfred the Great decreed that all free men's sons must learn to read and write English, and that gifted students should be sent to monasteries to study Latin. He established schools nationwide and translated Boethius's 'Consolation of Philosophy', Orosius's 'Histories against the Pagans', and other works, greatly advancing English culture.

v.t: Abbasids suppress the Zanj Rebellion
v.s: In 884, the Abbasid dynasty, allied with the Samanids of Persia, finally crushed the Zanj rebellion. The rebel leader Ali ibn Muhammad was killed, and the surviving black slaves were returned to their original homes. However, the economic damage to southern Mesopotamia was permanent; land was abandoned, and marshlands expanded.

v.t: Emperor Uda attempts to rule directly; Fujiwara still hold power
v.s: In 884, Emperor Uda came of age and tried to rule personally, but state affairs remained in the hands of Fujiwara no Mototsune. All imperial edicts required the chancellor's seal. This political system, with the emperor as a symbolic figure and the Fujiwara holding real power, became institutionalized.

v.t: Emperor Xizong returns to Chang'an
v.s: In March 885, after five years in exile, Emperor Xizong returned to Chang'an. But the city had been burned by Huang Chao; the palaces were in ruins, and grain prices soared. Xizong nevertheless indulged in feasting and favored eunuch Tian Lingzi. The Tang had lost control of most of the jiedushi.

v.t: Great Viking siege of Paris
v.s: In November 885, the Viking chieftain Sigfred led 700 ships and 30,000 men to besiege Paris. Count Odo of Paris and Bishop Gozlin defended the city for ten months. Eventually, King Charles the Fat of West Francia paid a ransom, and the Vikings withdrew. This was one of the most famous sieges in Parisian history.

v.t: Emperor Leo VI of Byzantium promulgates the 'Basilika'
v.s: In 885, Leo VI issued the complete 'Basilika' in 60 volumes. Written in Greek, it streamlined the Justinian Code, added Christian principles and new laws from the Macedonian dynasty, and represented the pinnacle of Byzantine legal systems.

v.t: Qin Zongquan proclaims himself emperor; chaos intensifies
v.s: In 885, Qin Zongquan, jiedushi of Caizhou, inherited the remnants of Huang Chao's forces and proclaimed himself emperor in Luoyang, adopting the state name 'Great Qi' and era name 'Longji'. His troops plundered widely, depopulating the Central Plains. Zhu Wen and Li Keyong united against him, and Qin was killed in 889.

v.t: Li Keyong and Zhu Wen begin their rivalry
v.s: In 886, Zhu Wen expanded his power from Bianzhou (present-day Kaifeng), while Li Keyong operated from Taiyuan. Conflicts over territory arose; Zhu Wen tried to assassinate Li Keyong at a banquet in Bianzhou, but Li escaped. The two became mortal enemies, fighting for over twenty years, with Zhu eventually emerging victorious.

v.t: Odo of West Francia crowned king
v.s: In 886, Count Odo of Paris, due to his leadership during the Viking siege, was elected king of West Francia by the nobles, and the Carolingian dynasty finally ended. Odo founded the Robertian house (later Capet), the true origin of the French monarchy.

v.t: Oleg of Kievan Rus' campaigns against Constantinople
v.s: In 886, Grand Prince Oleg led a fleet of 2,000 ships to Constantinople. Emperor Leo VI was forced to sue for peace, signing the Rus'-Byzantine Treaty, granting Rus' merchants duty-free trading rights in Constantinople and an annual tribute.

v.t: Tang eunuch Tian Lingzi expelled; Yang Fugong succeeds
v.s: In 886, Emperor Xizong came to favor the eunuch Yang Fugong, expelling Tian Lingzi. Yang Fugong proved even more corrupt, selling offices and titles, and the Tang central government lost all control over the provinces.

v.t: Yang Xingmi seizes Huainan, beginning the Ten Kingdoms
v.s: In 887, the prefect of Luzhou, Yang Xingmi, defeated his rivals and occupied Yangzhou. Emperor Zhaozong appointed him jiedushi of Huainan. Yang promoted peace and development, laying the foundation for the state of Wu, one of the Ten Kingdoms.

v.t: Arab Empire compiles administrative regulations
v.s: In 887, Caliph al-Mu'tadid ordered the compilation of the 'Administrative Regulations', standardizing provincial tax collection and official evaluation. Based on Sasanian administrative practices, it became an important Abbasid administrative document.

v.t: Fujiwara no Mototsune forces Emperor Uda to abdicate; installs Tokiyasu
v.s: In 887, Fujiwara no Mototsune forced Emperor Uda to abdicate and installed his grandson Tokiyasu as emperor. Mototsune continued as chancellor, and the emperor remained a puppet.

v.t: Alfred the Great signs the Treaty of Wedmore with the Vikings
v.s: In 887, Alfred the Great formally signed the Treaty of Wedmore with the Viking leader Guthrum, dividing England into two parts: the south under Wessex and the north (East Anglia and Northumbria) under Viking rule, the 'Danelaw'. The treaty brought a period of relative peace, allowing Wessex to recover.

v.t: Emperor Xizong dies; Zhaozong ascends
v.s: In March 888, Emperor Xizong died at age 27. His younger brother Li Ye ascended as Emperor Zhaozong. Young and ambitious, Zhaozong sought to restore Tang, reorganizing the imperial guards and curbing eunuch power. However, the Tang was already too far gone, and his efforts were doomed to fail.

v.t: Capetian dynasty formally established in West Francia
v.s: In 888, Count Odo of Paris died. The Carolingian Charles the Simple attempted to reclaim the throne, but the nobles elected Odo's brother Robert as king. The Capetian dynasty began to rule; in 987, Hugh Capet would formally establish the dynasty, which lasted until 1328.

v.t: Caliph al-Mu'tadid restores order in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 888, Caliph al-Mu'tadid, through military reforms and diplomacy, recovered some lost territories and reestablished Abbasid central authority. He moved the capital back to Baghdad, restored the House of Wisdom, and patronized scholars, prolonging the caliphate for nearly a century.

v.t: Kingdom of Nanzhao falls
v.s: In 888, Nanzhao, weakened by constant warfare and internal strife, was overthrown by the powerful minister Zheng Maisi, who established the Dachanghe kingdom. Nanzhao had lasted 250 years, and its successor would eventually become the Dali kingdom.

v.t: Zhu Wen destroys Qin Zongquan, dominates Central Plains
v.s: In 889, Zhu Wen destroyed Qin Zongquan, sending him to Chang'an for execution. Zhu absorbed Caizhou, Luoyang, and other areas, becoming the most powerful jiedushi in the Central Plains. Zhaozong granted him the title Prince of Liang, but Zhu no longer respected the court.

v.t: Fujiwara regency officially established in Japan
v.s: In 889, Fujiwara no Mototsune was formally granted the title 'Sesshō' (regent), the highest office in Japan. Thereafter, the Fujiwara clan monopolized the positions of regent and chancellor, and the emperor was a figurehead until the end of the Heian period.

v.t: Oleg of Kievan Rus' conquers the Derevlian tribe
v.s: In 889, Oleg conquered the Derevlians, a tribe near Kiev, forcing them to pay tribute in furs and honey. He expanded Kievan Rus' to the entire middle Dnieper region, making it a major East European power.

v.t: Byzantine navy recovers Crete
v.s: In 889, the Byzantine admiral Himerios led a fleet to recover Crete, which had been under Arab control for over a century, and established a theme there. Crete became an important naval base for the Byzantine Empire.

v.t: Zhaozong campaigns against Li Keyong and fails
v.s: In 890, Emperor Zhaozong united with Zhu Wen, Li Kuangwei, and other jiedushi against Li Keyong. Due to infighting, the government forces were routed. Li Keyong led his Shatuo cavalry into Jinzhou, threatening Chang'an. Zhaozong was forced to recognize Li Keyong as Prince of Jin, and the court's prestige was completely lost.

v.t: Great Moravia accepts Christianity
v.s: In 890, King Svatopluk of Great Moravia officially accepted Orthodox Christianity, inviting Byzantine missionaries to establish a church organization. However, pagans remained numerous, and Christianization was slow.

v.t: Japan abolishes the envoy missions to Tang
v.s: In 890, due to Tang's internal chaos and maritime risks, the Japanese court formally ended the practice of sending missions to Tang. The 300-year system of kentōshi (imperial embassies to China) came to an end. Japan turned to developing its own 'national culture', and kana literature began to flourish.

v.t: Samanids unify Transoxiana
v.s: In 890, the Samanid ruler Ismail Samani defeated the Saffarids and captured Khorasan, unifying Transoxiana. He encouraged agriculture, trade, and the patronage of Islam; Bukhara became a cultural center of Central Asia.

v.t: Wang Jian seizes Shu, establishing the Former Shu
v.s: In 891, Wang Jian, prefect of Bizhou, captured Chengdu and was appointed jiedushi of Xichuan by Zhaozong. Wang recruited talented men and promoted agriculture, laying the foundation for the Former Shu kingdom; he would formally proclaim himself emperor nine years later.

v.t: Death of Alfred the Great
v.s: In 891 (actual 899), Alfred the Great died; his son Edward the Elder succeeded. Edward continued the fight against the Vikings and by 927 completed the conquest of the Danelaw, unifying England.

v.t: Zhu Wen and Li Keyong clash again
v.s: In 891, Zhu Wen and Li Keyong fought a major battle at Luzhou (present-day Changzhi, Shanxi) for control of Hebei. Li Keyong defeated Zhu and captured Luzhou. Thereafter, the two fought annually, leaving the Guanzhong region devastated.

v.t: Yasovarman I completes the East Baray reservoir
v.s: In 891, the East Baray and its associated canals were completed, turning Angkor into a metropolis with a sophisticated water management system. Yasovarman I also built over a hundred temples throughout the Angkor region.

v.t: Dong Chang proclaims himself emperor; Qian Liu campaigns against him
v.s: In 892, the jiedushi of eastern Zhejiang, Dong Chang, proclaimed himself emperor in Yuezhou (present-day Shaoxing) with the state name 'Da Yue Luoping'. Qian Liu, jiedushi of Zhenhai, was ordered by Zhaozong to attack; the following year he captured Yuezhou and executed Dong Chang. Qian Liu was granted the title Prince of Wuyue, becoming the founder of the Wuyue kingdom.

v.t: Magyars invade Italy
v.s: In 892, Magyar horsemen crossed the Alps and plundered northern Italy, reaching as far as Rome. Pope John IX organized a coalition against them, but the Magyars were fast and elusive; European powers were unable to resist effectively.

v.t: Qarmatian revolt in the Arab Empire
v.s: In 892, the Qarmatians, a branch of Shiism, launched an uprising in southern Iraq, establishing a communal theocracy. They attacked the Abbasids, raided Mecca and pillaged the Black Stone. The Qarmatian movement lasted into the 10th century.

v.t: Tang eunuch Yang Fugong is demoted and commits suicide
v.s: In 892, Emperor Zhaozong allied with Chancellor Xu Yanruo to eliminate the eunuch Yang Fugong. Yang fled to Hanzhong and raised an army, but was defeated by Li Maozhen and committed suicide. Zhaozong regained some power, but the jiedushi problem became even worse.

v.t: Qian Liu appointed jiedushi of Zhenhai and Zhendong, controlling the two Zhejiangs
v.s: In 893, Emperor Zhaozong appointed Qian Liu as jiedushi of both Zhenhai and Zhendong circuits, governing modern Zhejiang and southern Jiangsu. Qian built the city walls of Hangzhou, improved water conservancy, and maintained peace and security; the Wuyue kingdom became the most stable area during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

v.t: Bulgarian Tsar Simeon I attacks Byzantium
v.s: In 893, Tsar Simeon I of Bulgaria, citing Byzantine violation of a trade treaty, invaded Thrace and routed the Byzantine army. Byzantium sued for peace, agreeing to pay annual tribute. Simeon aspired to seize Constantinople.

v.t: Sugawara no Michizane appointed Minister of the Right in Japan
v.s: In 893, the scholar Sugawara no Michizane was appointed Minister of the Right (Udaijin), one of the few non-Fujiwara to hold high office. He introduced political reforms and curbed aristocratic luxury, but fell victim to Fujiwara intrigue and was exiled to Dazaifu.

v.t: Oleg of Kievan Rus' dies; Igor succeeds
v.s: In 893, Oleg died (actual 912). His successor Igor continued expansion but lacked Oleg's abilities, and Kievan Rus' entered a period of relative stagnation.

v.t: Japan formally abolishes the kentōshi system
v.s: In 894, Emperor Uda, acting on the advice of Sugawara no Michizane, formally abolished the missions to Tang. Japan turned to developing its own culture; kana script became widespread, and waka poetry and monogatari literature flourished. Official Sino-Japanese exchanges were interrupted for over 500 years.

v.t: Simeon I proclaims himself tsar
v.s: In 894, after defeating the Byzantine army, Simeon I proclaimed himself 'Tsar of the Bulgarians and Greeks' and demanded that Byzantium recognize his imperial title. Byzantium was forced to do so nominally, marking the beginning of Bulgaria as an empire.

v.t: Li Maozhen of Tang attacks Chang'an, burning palaces
v.s: In 894, the jiedushi of Fengxiang, Li Maozhen, attacked Chang'an, burning the imperial palace. Emperor Zhaozong fled to Shimen. Li Keyong led his army to the rescue, defeated Li Maozhen, and Zhaozong returned to Chang'an. The capital was in ruins, and Zhaozong became a pawn of the jiedushi.

v.t: Samanids defeat the Saffarids
v.s: In 894, the Samanid army captured the Saffarid capital Shiraz and took its ruler prisoner. The Samanids unified eastern Persia and Central Asia, becoming the strongest vassal of the Abbasid caliphate.

v.t: Three jiedushi rebel; Zhaozong flees to Huazhou
v.s: In 895, the three jiedushi of Fengxiang, Jingnan, and Zhenguo (Li Maozhen, Wang Xingyu, Han Jian) joined forces to attack Chang'an. Zhaozong fled to Huazhou (present-day Hua County, Shaanxi). Li Keyong led his army to the rescue, defeating the coalition and killing Wang Xingyu. Zhaozong returned to Chang'an but had completely lost authority, relying on Li Keyong's protection.

v.t: Árpád unifies the Magyar tribes
v.s: In 895, the seven Magyar chieftains elected Árpád as their grand prince, unifying the tribes. He led the Magyars to settle in the Carpathian Basin, founding the Kingdom of Hungary. The Árpád dynasty would last until 1301.

v.t: Death of Arab physician al-Razi
v.s: In 895, the famous Arab physician al-Razi died in Baghdad. He wrote over 200 works, emphasizing clinical observation and experiment, and was called the 'Galen of the Arabs'. His 'Kitab al-Hawi' was used as a medical textbook in Europe until the 17th century.

v.t: Yasovarman I of Angkor dies; Harshavarman I succeeds
v.s: In 895, Yasovarman I died and was succeeded by his son Harshavarman I. The Angkor dynasty continued to expand, conquering the lower Mekong region and reaching the border of Champa (central Vietnam).

v.t: Qian Liu builds the outer city of Hangzhou, founding Wuyue
v.s: In 896, Qian Liu constructed the outer wall of Hangzhou, with a circumference of 70 li and ten gates. He dredged West Lake and built the Qiantang River seawall, making Hangzhou a major southeastern metropolis. He also established a dredging corps to maintain waterways and develop agriculture.

v.t: Death of Alfred the Great
v.s: In 896 (actual 899), Alfred the Great died, succeeded by his son Edward the Elder. Edward continued to fight the Vikings and by 927 had conquered the Danelaw, unifying England.

v.t: Ma Yin seizes Hunan, establishes the Chu kingdom
v.s: In 896, Ma Yin, jiedushi of Wuan, occupied Tanzhou (present-day Changsha) and was granted the title Prince of Chu by Zhaozong. Ma promoted the tea-horse trade, using Hunan tea to exchange for war horses from the Central Plains; the Chu state became an important power during the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.

v.t: Byzantine Empire recovers Cyprus
v.s: In 896, the Byzantine navy defeated an Arab fleet and recaptured the island of Cyprus, restoring imperial rule. Byzantium rebuilt churches and fortifications, making Cyprus an important eastern bulwark of the empire.

v.t: Eunuch Han Quanhui monopolizes power, controls Zhaozong
v.s: In 897, the eunuch Han Quanhui replaced Yang Fugong as commander of the Shence Army. He placed Zhaozong under house arrest, forced him to appoint a crown prince, and allied with Li Maozhen against Zhu Wen. The Tang central government had become a puppet of eunuchs and jiedushi.

v.t: Zhu Wen defeats Li Keyong, occupies most of Hebei
v.s: In 897, Zhu Wen defeated Li Keyong at the Battle of Huanshui, occupying Weibo, Chengde, and other circuits. Li Keyong retreated to Taiyuan with greatly reduced strength. Zhu Wen became the most powerful jiedushi in the north and began planning to usurp the Tang throne.

v.t: Fujiwara no Tokihira appointed Minister of the Left in Japan
v.s: In 897, Fujiwara no Tokihira was appointed Minister of the Left (Sadaijin) and took charge of the government. He falsely accused Sugawara no Michizane of treason, exiling him to Dazaifu. The Fujiwara suppressed all opposition, and the regency entered its darkest period.

v.t: Samanids conquer Khwarazm
v.s: In 897, Ismail Samani of the Samanids conquered Khwarazm, extending the realm to the Aral Sea. The Islamization of Central Asia deepened, and Khwarazm later became a center of Islamic culture.

v.t: Zhu Wen swallows up Hebei circuits, greatly increasing his power
v.s: In 898, Zhu Wen absorbed the Hebei circuits of Lulong, Chengde, and Yiwu, expanding his sphere of influence north of the Yellow River. His power now exceeded that of Li Keyong, and he began to control the court.

v.t: Sugawara no Michizane exiled to Dazaifu
v.s: In 898, Sugawara no Michizane, falsely accused by Fujiwara no Tokihira, was demoted to Dazaifu (present-day Fukuoka) by Emperor Daigo. He died in exile two years later, but after his death he was deified as the god of learning and enshrined at Dazaifu Tenmangu Shrine.

v.t: Bulgarian Tsar Simeon I captures Thessaloniki
v.s: In 898, Tsar Simeon I of Bulgaria captured Thessaloniki, the second largest city of Byzantium, and took high-ranking Byzantine officers prisoner. Byzantium was forced to cede territory and sue for peace, making Bulgaria the dominant Balkan power.

v.t: Caliph al-Muktafi pacifies the Qarmatians
v.s: In 898, Caliph al-Muktafi took advantage of divisions within the Qarmatians, allied with Persian forces, and suppressed them. The remaining Qarmatians retreated to Bahrain, where they established an independent Shiite state.

v.t: Zhu Wen besieges Taiyuan; Li Keyong nearly destroyed
v.s: In 899, Zhu Wen laid siege to Taiyuan with a large army. Li Keyong held out for months, running out of food and reinforcements. However, heavy rains caused disease to break out in Zhu's army, forcing him to withdraw. Although Li Keyong saved Taiyuan, his power was greatly diminished, and he could no longer contend with Zhu Wen.

v.t: Normans colonize the mouth of the Seine
v.s: In 899, the Norman chieftain Rollo established a permanent base at the mouth of the Seine and built the city of Rouen. He accepted baptism, was granted the title Duke of Normandy, and the Duchy of Normandy formally came into being, later becoming the base for the conquest of England.

v.t: Jayavarman IV usurps the throne of Angkor
v.s: In 899, the Angkorian general Jayavarman IV usurped the throne, killing Harshavarman I. He moved the capital to Koh Ker and built a seven-tiered pyramid temple 36 meters high. Under Jayavarman IV, the Angkor kingdom fell into a brief period of fragmentation.

v.t: Arab philosopher al-Farabi moves to Baghdad
v.s: In 899, al-Farabi left his homeland and traveled to the House of Wisdom in Baghdad for advanced study. He studied logic, music, and philosophy, wrote the 'Great Book of Music', and commented on Aristotle's works. Al-Farabi later became a foundational figure in Islamic philosophy.

v.t: Eunuchs imprison Zhaozong; crown prince enthroned
v.s: In November 900, the eunuch Liu Jishu staged a coup, imprisoning Emperor Zhaozong in the Shaoyang courtyard and sealing the door with molten iron. He issued a false edict enthroning the crown prince Li Yu as emperor, making Zhaozong a retired emperor. Zhu Wen led his army to punish the eunuchs; Liu Jishu was killed, and Zhaozong was restored. From then on, Zhaozong was completely under Zhu Wen's control.

v.t: Peak of Córdoba under the Umayyads
v.s: In 900, under Caliph Abd al-Rahman III (891-961), Córdoba became the largest city in Europe, with 70 libraries, hundreds of bathhouses, paved streets, and street lighting. The University of Córdoba attracted Christian, Jewish, and Muslim scholars, making it a cultural beacon of Europe.

v.t: End of the Classic Maya period
v.s: In 900, the last Classic Maya city-states (Uxmal, Chichen Itza) ceased to erect dated stelae, marking the definitive end of Classic Maya civilization. The collapse was caused by a combination of drought, environmental degradation, war, and trade disruption. Postclassic Maya cities (such as Mayapan) were smaller and less culturally advanced.

v.t: Magyars continue to raid Europe
v.s: In 900, Magyar horsemen raided Bavaria, Saxony, and Italy, reaching as far as northern Spain. Using composite bows and light cavalry tactics, they were difficult for European knights to counter. It was not until their defeat at the Battle of Lechfeld in 955 by Otto the Great that the Magyars stopped their raids.

v.t: Rise of the Ife Kingdom and Yoruba city-states
v.s: Circa 900-1800, Ife became the religious and political center of the Yoruba people. Ife is famous for its naturalistic brass and bronze sculptures, representing the pinnacle of African art. The Yoruba developed a dense network of city-states (e.g., Oyo) with complex political systems and a pantheon of orisha deities. Ife art profoundly influenced the later bronze tradition of the Kingdom of Benin.

v.t: Nazca Lines drawn in the Peruvian desert
v.s: Circa 200 BC – 700 AD, the Nazca people of the southern Peruvian coast created giant geoglyphs (the Nazca Lines) on the desert floor, including hummingbirds, spiders, monkeys, and geometric shapes. The largest figures are up to 370 meters long and can only be fully appreciated from the air. They may have been used for religious rituals and astronomical observation. The Nazca also built underground aqueducts (puquios) for irrigation in the arid desert.

v.t: Toltec civilization and the city of Tula
v.s: Circa 900-1168, the Toltecs established an empire in central Mexico, with their capital at Tula (Tollan), famous for its giant warrior columns. The Toltecs conquered much of central Mexico, and their cultural influence reached as far as the Maya region (Chichen Itza displays a distinctly Toltec style). The cult of Quetzalcoatl (the Feathered Serpent) reached its peak under the Toltecs. Tula was destroyed by northern nomadic tribes around 1168, and the Toltec empire collapsed.

v.t: Rise of the Chimú Empire on the northern Peruvian coast
v.s: Circa 900-1470, the Chimú Empire, with its capital at Chan Chan (near present-day Trujillo, Peru), developed the largest adobe city in the Andes (covering about 20 square kilometers). The Chimú were expert metallurgists, skilled in gold, silver, and copper alloys. The empire had an estimated population of 300,000 and built an extensive irrigation system. It was conquered by the Inca Empire in 1470, and its artisans were forcibly relocated to Cusco.

v.t: Chavín culture: the earliest pan-Andean religious center
v.s: Circa 900-200 BC, Chavín de Huántar (in the Peruvian Andes) became the first pan-Andean religious center. The Chavín built a large U-shaped temple complex with internal labyrinthine stone galleries. The famous 'Lanzón' stela depicts a deity that is half-human and half-jaguar. The Chavín artistic style (characterized by anthropomorphic jaguars, snakes, and eagles) spread throughout the Andes, representing the first unified religious tradition in the region.
